q: In CBS, what is a Table (or Application)?
options: A spreadsheet visible to the end user
A fundamental system entity governed by a centralized Table Registry
A backup file of customer records
tip: A Table in CBS is the fundamental logical entity that holds banking records — think of it like a schema/container for one type of object (CUSTOMER, ACCOUNT, etc.). Its execution, data structures, and physical storage are all controlled by a central system index called the Table Registry, which acts as the authoritative service directory.

q: What governs the execution, data structures, and physical storage of a Table?
tip: The Table Registry is the central, immutable index that governs every Table in CBS: it controls which programs can run, where data is stored, how it's classified, and how multi-company segregation is enforced. Without the Registry, a Table cannot operate — the system rejects any unregistered program or data definition.

q: What is the role of the Table Registry?
options: The system's service directory enforcing authorization and multi-company segregation
tip: The Table Registry acts as the system's service directory. It enforces TWO key controls: (1) authorization — only programs registered in the Registry may execute; (2) multi-company segregation — data scoping rules ensure one company's transactional data cannot leak into another's reports or balances.

q: A Table contains records related to which kind of object?
tip: A Table represents one type of banking object — like CUSTOMER (party data), ACCOUNT (balances), or FUNDS.TRANSFER (payments). Each Table holds many Records (instances) and defines the data model, business rules, access controls, and integration points for that object type.

q: Which of the following is NOT defined by a Table?
tip: Tables define the LOGICAL banking object — its data model, business processing rules, access controls, and integration points. They don't manage hardware-level concerns like disk firmware or networking; those belong to the underlying database and OS layers.

q: How are Tables categorized?
options: By functionality and how they handle financial data
By the user who created them
tip: Tables are categorized by their functional role (Customer/Entity, Account/Balance, Transactional, Loans/Deposits, System Config) AND by their financial classification (INT for installation-wide parameters, FIN for transactional financial data, CUS for customer data). Both axes together determine how a Table is used.

q: Which Table stores master data for individuals or corporates?
tip: The CUSTOMER Table holds the master data record for each individual or corporate client — fields like SHORT.NAME (display name), SECTOR (business segment), TARGET (customer target group), NATIONALITY. Each new client gets a unique Customer ID that ties together all their accounts, loans, and contracts.

q: What are key fields in the CUSTOMER table?
tip: Key Fields on the CUSTOMER Table include SHORT.NAME (display name), SECTOR (e.g. retail vs corporate), TARGET (sub-segment for marketing/reporting), and NATIONALITY. These attributes drive how the customer is treated downstream — different sectors may get different products, pricing, or compliance treatment.

q: What is the USER table used for?
options: Internal staff management (login credentials, access rights)
tip: The USER Table represents internal bank STAFF (not customers) — tellers, managers, back-office staff. It holds login credentials, role assignments, allowed departments, and access rights. Don't confuse it with CUSTOMER (which holds clients of the bank). Audit fields INPUTTER/AUTHORISER reference USER IDs.

q: Which classification typically applies to the CUSTOMER table?
tip: Classification controls data scope. CUS (Customer) — tables holding client-related data, which may be shared or segregated by configuration; INT (Installation) — tables shared across all companies, like LANGUAGE; FIN (Financial) — strictly segregated transactional data per company.

q: Which table maintains current, savings, or internal GL balances?
tip: The ACCOUNT Table holds the current state of each banking account — balances, currency, the linked CUSTOMER, the CATEGORY (product type). It's the 'money living somewhere' Table: when a deposit happens, the WORKING.BALANCE field on the relevant ACCOUNT Record is updated.

q: Which field on ACCOUNT updates when a customer deposits cash?
tip: When a customer deposits cash, the system updates the WORKING.BALANCE Field on their ACCOUNT Record — this is the current real-time balance available for transactions. The deposit transaction itself is captured in FUNDS.TRANSFER or TELLER; the ACCOUNT's running balance reflects the cumulative effect.

q: What does the CATEGORY table define?
tip: The CATEGORY Table is the configuration table that defines and groups products. For example, you might configure ranges: accounts numbered 1000-1999 are 'Current Accounts', 6000-6999 are 'Savings Accounts'. CATEGORY drives reporting, accounting, and product behavior — without it, the bank wouldn't know what kind of account each ACCOUNT Record represents.

q: Account range 1000-1999 in CATEGORY might represent…
tip: Account numbering ranges in the CATEGORY Table classify accounts by product. The textbook example: 1000-1999 = Current Accounts, 6000-6999 = Savings Accounts. CATEGORY is what lets reports group accounts by product family without each account having to store its product type as a string.

q: FUNDS.TRANSFER table is used to…
tip: FUNDS.TRANSFER is the transactional Table for moving money. It supports Internal transfers (between own accounts), SWIFT (international), and Local (domestic clearing). Each transfer Record specifies a Debit Account (where the money comes FROM), a Credit Account (where it goes TO), the amount, and the transaction type.

q: Which fields are key to FUNDS.TRANSFER?
tip: Key Fields on FUNDS.TRANSFER: DEBIT.ACCT.NO (source account), CREDIT.ACCT.NO (destination), DEBIT.AMOUNT (the value being moved), TRANSACTION.TYPE (Internal/SWIFT/Local). Together they capture the essential mechanics of any payment.

q: What is the TELLER table used for?
tip: The TELLER Table records over-the-counter cash operations — cash withdrawals, deposits, currency exchanges done at a branch counter. When a customer walks in to withdraw USD 500, the teller uses this Table to record the cash-out and update vault balances. Distinct from FUNDS.TRANSFER, which is for account-to-account movements.

q: What does AA.ARRANGEMENT manage?
options: The lifecycle of a specific loan or deposit contract
tip: AA.ARRANGEMENT (Arrangement Architecture Arrangement) is the Table that manages the LIFECYCLE of a specific loan or deposit contract. It ties together the interest rates, repayment schedule, fees, and penalty rules into one coherent Arrangement — the bank's record of 'this is John's specific 20-year mortgage'. It's the CBS equivalent of a Temenos ARRANGEMENT.

q: A 20-year mortgage would be tracked in which table?
tip: A 20-year mortgage is tracked as a single Arrangement Record in AA.ARRANGEMENT. The Arrangement ties together interest rates, the repayment schedule, late-payment penalties, collateral references — the full lifecycle of that specific contract. The Product (template) and Customer are referenced; the Arrangement is the specific instance.

q: Which table controls the Bank Date?
tip: The DATES Table controls the system's Bank Date — the date the system considers 'today' for processing. It can differ from the calendar date (e.g. during long batch cycles). It works with the HOLIDAY Table to skip weekends/holidays when advancing the date during End-of-Day (EOD) processing.

q: What does the DATES table reference to decide whether interest should accrue?
tip: DATES looks up HOLIDAY when deciding whether to roll forward. If today's calendar date is a Saturday or a bank holiday, DATES won't advance the Bank Date through it — interest doesn't accrue, scheduled payments are postponed, etc. This is why batch interest calculations correctly handle non-working days without bespoke code.

q: Which table defines ISO currency codes and exchange-rate decimals?
tip: The CURRENCY Table defines ISO codes (USD, EUR, JPY...), the number of allowed decimal places per currency (USD = 2, JPY = 0), and exchange rate metadata. Any amount Field validated against CURRENCY automatically respects the right decimal rule, preventing things like 0.01 JPY transactions.

q: In CURRENCY, which property prevents JPY from having decimal places?
tip: CURRENCY defines the decimal places allowed per ISO currency. JPY = 0 (yen has no sub-units), USD = 2 (cents). When a Field is linked to CURRENCY, the system reads this metadata to validate inputs — entering 100.50 JPY would fail; 100.50 USD is fine. This is hot-swap config, not code.

q: Internal staff and their department access rights are managed by which table?
tip: Internal staff management is the USER Table's job. It defines a teller's or manager's identity, allowed departments, access rights, and password. CUSTOMER is for clients of the bank; USER is for employees of the bank. The two are referenced separately by audit fields (USER) and contracts (CUSTOMER).

q: The CUSTOMER ID created at onboarding is used to…
options: Link future accounts and loans to that customer
tip: The unique Customer ID is the primary key of the CUSTOMER Table. Once assigned during onboarding, it's the anchor for every downstream relationship: ACCOUNT Records reference it; AA.ARRANGEMENT (loans/deposits) reference it; FUNDS.TRANSFER Records may reference it. Without that ID, there's nothing to link the customer's activities together.

q: What is the foundation of every Table in CBS?
options: Mandatory registration in the system index
tip: Every Table must be registered in the system's central index BEFORE it can be used. This mandatory registration is what enables central control over execution and storage — without a Registry entry, the Table can't operate at all. The Registry has two parts: PROGRAM (executables) and FILE (physical storage).

q: Which registry governs the executable artifacts associated with a Table?
tip: The PROGRAM Registry is the index of executable artifacts — every program/script that can run in the system must have an active record here. This is a security control: the system strictly blocks the execution of any program lacking a Registry record, preventing unauthorized or rogue code from running.

q: What happens if a program is NOT registered in the PROGRAM registry?
options: The system strictly blocks its execution
tip: Strict gateway enforcement: if a program isn't in the PROGRAM Registry, it does NOT run. The system refuses to execute it. This is a hard control to prevent unauthorized code execution — it's why all custom Plugins (Java/jBase) must be registered before they can be invoked from Projections.

q: Which registry determines the Table's physical database location?
tip: The FILE Registry holds the metadata for every Table's physical storage — what kind of files it has (LIV, NAU, HIS, etc.), where they live, how they behave operationally, and how the data is classified (INT/FIN/CUS). Without a FILE Registry entry, the Table is non-existent to the system.

q: What does the FILE registry define besides location?
tip: Besides physical location, the FILE Registry defines the Table's operational behavior (e.g. is it transactional, batch-friendly, etc.) and its classification — INT (Installation, globally shared), FIN (Financial, segregated per company), or CUS (Customer data). These three facts together govern how the Table is treated everywhere.

q: What is the purpose of the CLASSIFICATION attribute?
tip: CLASSIFICATION is a Table-level attribute set in the FILE Registry. Its purpose is to enforce strict multi-company data segregation — ensuring that, for example, Company A's accounts are completely isolated from Company B's, while shared parameters (like CURRENCY) remain available to both. Without CLASSIFICATION, data segregation would be ad-hoc and error-prone.

q: INT classification means…
options: Installation: stores global parameters shared across all companies
tip: INT = Installation classification. INT Tables hold global parameters shared across ALL companies in the installation — e.g. COUNTRY codes, LANGUAGE codes, CURRENCY definitions. One copy of the data; every company sees it. Compare with FIN (Financial — strictly per-company) and CUS (Customer — configurable).

q: FIN classification stores…
options: Transactional data segregated by the lead company or book
tip: FIN = Financial classification. FIN Tables hold transactional data that MUST be segregated per company/book — e.g. ACCOUNT balances, FUNDS.TRANSFER records. This is a non-negotiable regulatory and accounting requirement: one company cannot see another's books.

q: CUS classification is used for…
options: Customer client data, which may be shared or segregated per config
tip: CUS = Customer classification. CUS Tables hold client data which may be either shared across companies or segregated per company, depending on the bank's configuration. This flexibility lets banking groups share a single customer view across subsidiaries when desired, or keep customers segregated by company when regulation requires.

q: COUNTRY and LANGUAGE tables would most likely be classified as…
tip: COUNTRY and LANGUAGE Tables hold globally-shared parameters — every company in the installation uses the same country codes and language codes. They're classified as INT (Installation), the classification reserved for installation-wide parameters that don't vary by company.

q: ACCOUNT and FUNDS.TRANSFER are classified as…
tip: ACCOUNT (balances) and FUNDS.TRANSFER (payments) are transactional financial data. They're classified as FIN (Financial), meaning they're strictly segregated by the lead company or book. Each company keeps its own copies; nothing leaks across — required for separate accounting and regulatory reporting.

q: To resolve physical storage, a Table is linked to…
options: A MODULE code (e.g. AC or ST)
tip: Every Table is linked to a MODULE code (e.g. AC for Accounts, ST for Static data) which determines the Table's directory path inside the database. So the Table's physical location isn't arbitrary — it's resolved from the MODULE code via the FILE Registry. This systematic naming keeps the database organized.

q: How is the physical structure of a Table partitioned?
options: Into distinct lifecycle spaces (LIV, NAU, HIS, DEL, SIM)
tip: The Table's physical structure is partitioned into distinct lifecycle SPACES (a.k.a. file types): LIV (Live), NAU (Unauthorized drafts), HIS (History), DEL (Deleted drafts), and SIM (Simulation). Each space holds the same record ID in a different state — this is what enables strict data integrity and audit trails.

q: LIV (Live) is a…
tip: LIV = Live. It holds the currently active, authorized version of every Record. LIV is a SINGLETON file: only ONE record per ID. So for CUSTOMER 123456, LIV holds exactly one current state — the authorized truth. The other lifecycle spaces (NAU, HIS) hold other states for the same ID.

q: NAU is a…
options: Singleton acting as transaction buffer for uncommitted drafts
tip: NAU = Unauthorized. It's the buffer file that holds in-flight changes: HOLD drafts (incomplete, unvalidated) and UNAU records (completed edits awaiting second-user approval). NAU is also a SINGLETON: only one record per ID. This is the key concurrency-control mechanism — if a NAU record exists for an ID, no one else can edit it.

q: HIS supports…
options: Many records per ID, with sequential suffixes (;1, ;2, ;3)
tip: HIS = History. Unlike LIV and NAU which are singletons, HIS supports MULTIPLE records per ID — each historical version gets a sequential suffix (ID;1, ID;2, ID;3, ...). HIS is the immutable archive: when a record is amended and the amendment is authorized, the OLD live version is copy-on-write archived into HIS.

q: DEL file stores…
tip: DEL is the singleton file for deleted unauthorized records. Specifically: if a brand-new draft Record (SEQ.NO=1) is deleted from NAU before being authorized, it's moved to DEL to preserve a forensic audit trail. This way, even data that was input and then canceled is preserved for compliance.

q: SIM is used for…
tip: SIM = Simulation. It holds 'what-if' data — completely isolated from active financial records. SIM is where stress tests, scenario analyses, and proposal calculations live. Records in SIM cannot generate accounting entries or affect any other lifecycle file — perfect for risk analyses without contaminating real books.

q: What appears at the end of every Table's data structure?
tip: Every Table's data structure ends with a block of system-maintained AUDIT FIELDS (read-only). These include: RECORD.STATUS (e.g. UNAU, HOLD, LIVE, REVE), INPUTTER (multi-valued user IDs who created/edited each version), AUTHORISER (multi-valued user IDs who approved each version), DATE.TIME (timestamps), and COMPANY (where each action happened).

q: RECORD.STATUS represents…
options: The current operational state (UNAU, HOLD, LIVE, REVE)
tip: RECORD.STATUS is the audit Field that records the operational state of each Record. Values include: HOLD (unvalidated draft), UNAU (pending authorization), LIVE (currently active), REVE (reversed/canceled). The status drives which lifecycle file the Record lives in: HOLD/UNAU live in NAU; LIVE lives in LIV; REVE lives in HIS.

q: INPUTTER and AUTHORISER are…
options: Multi-valued arrays of User ID and Department
tip: INPUTTER and AUTHORISER are MULTI-VALUED audit Fields — arrays, not scalars. Each entry captures one cycle (who created the v1, who approved v1, who edited v2, who approved v2, etc.). They capture the user ID + department of every staff who created/modified vs approved the Record. Critical for fraud investigations.

q: DATE.TIME audit field tracks…
options: Exact timestamps for every input and authorization
tip: DATE.TIME is the audit Field that captures the exact timestamps of every input and authorization event on a Record. It's multi-valued alongside INPUTTER/AUTHORISER. Combined with COMPANY (also multi-valued), it tells you not just 'who and what' but 'where and when' every change happened.

q: COMPANY audit field stores…
options: The specific companies where the actions were made
tip: COMPANY is an audit Field capturing which specific company/legal entity each action took place in. Important in multi-company installations — the same USER might work across Company A and Company B; the audit trail must show which company's books were touched. Multi-valued alongside INPUTTER/DATE.TIME.

q: Audit fields are…
tip: Audit Fields (RECORD.STATUS, INPUTTER, AUTHORISER, DATE.TIME, COMPANY) are SYSTEM-MAINTAINED and READ-ONLY. Users cannot write to them directly. The system automatically updates them during INPUT, AUTH, REVERSE, and DELETE operations to preserve an unfalsifiable forensic trail. Tampering with them would defeat the audit framework.

q: The MODULE code (e.g. AC, ST) controls…
options: The directory path within the database structure
tip: MODULE codes (e.g. AC = Accounts, ST = Static) define WHERE in the database directory tree a Table's physical files live. So a Table linked to MODULE AC is stored in the AC directory path; ST goes elsewhere. This systematic placement keeps the database organized and enables efficient backup/recovery.

q: Which file represents the current authoritative state of a record?
tip: LIV (Live) holds the current, fully authorized state of a Record. It's the primary source of truth for all the system's operational calculations — interest accrual, balance lookups, reporting, ledger postings. When a record is in LIV, it's 'live' in the bank's books.

q: How many records can the LIV file hold per ID?
tip: LIV is a SINGLETON file — strictly ONE record per ID. This means there's only one 'live' version of CUSTOMER 123456 at any time. If you need to see older versions, they're in HIS. If a draft is pending, it's in NAU. LIV holds only the current authorized state.

q: What does the NAU file store?
tip: The NAU (Unauthorized) file is a BUFFER. It holds work-in-progress: HOLD records (incomplete drafts the user saved to resume later, unvalidated) and UNAU records (completed edits awaiting second-user approval per the 4-Eyes Principle). NAU records are not yet in the books — they can't post to the GL.

q: Can records in NAU generate accounting entries?
tip: Records in NAU are 'Financially Isolated' — they cannot generate accounting entries or affect the General Ledger. This is by design: NAU holds DRAFTS and PENDING records, not authorized truth. Only when a NAU record is AUTH'd does it move to LIV and become eligible to post to the ledger.

q: Can a record be in both HOLD and UNAU simultaneously in NAU?
options: No — cardinality constraint, only one record per ID
tip: NAU is a SINGLETON file: only ONE record per ID at a time. Because of this cardinality constraint, a single record can't be in both UNAU (awaiting authorization) AND HOLD (unvalidated draft) simultaneously — the file would have nowhere to put two records under the same ID.

q: What does the system do if a user tries to edit a LIV record that already has an active NAU draft by someone else?
tip: Edit locking via NAU prevents concurrent modification. If Alice has a draft of CUSTOMER 123456 sitting in NAU, and Bob tries to edit the same record, NAU has no room for a second draft (it's a singleton). The system returns 'Record Locked' so Alice's pending work isn't overwritten — built-in concurrency control.

q: What kind of structure is HIS?
tip: HIS (History) is the immutable archive of past versions. Unlike LIV and NAU which are singletons, HIS supports MANY records per ID — each historical version is suffixed (ID;1, ID;2, ID;3). When a Record is amended and authorized, the old version is copy-on-write moved into HIS. The chain in HIS is permanent and read-only.

q: What triggers the move of a LIV record into HIS?
tip: Copy-on-Write: when an amendment to a Record is AUTH'd (authorized), the system FIRST moves the old LIV version to HIS (giving it the next suffix, e.g. ID;n), THEN promotes the new authorized version into LIV. This way HIS accumulates every prior version automatically — no manual archiving needed.

q: How are historical records distinguished?
tip: Historical versions in HIS are distinguished by sequential suffixes: ID;1 (oldest archived), ID;2, ID;3, ... The HIGHEST suffix represents the most-recently archived version (the last LIV state before the current one). This lets auditors retrieve specific historical states by ID;n.

q: What is HISTORY_RESTORE used for?
options: Retrieving a specific HIS version and reinstating it as the active record
tip: HISTORY_RESTORE is the operation that retrieves a specific past version from HIS and reinstates it as the current Record. For FIN/CUS Tables, restoration goes through a HNAU (History Restore Not Authorized) review-and-approve cycle in NAU; for INT Tables it can overwrite LIV directly. Useful for reversing an erroneous later amendment.

q: DEL is best described as…
options: A singleton for deleted unauthorized copies
tip: DEL holds DELETED UNAUTHORIZED records. Specifically: if a brand-new Record (SEQ.NO=1) was input but then deleted from NAU before ever being authorized, DEL preserves it for forensic audit. DEL is a SINGLETON (one record per ID) and exists purely to retain evidence of canceled drafts.

q: When a brand-new record (SEQ.NO=1) is deleted from NAU, what happens?
options: It is moved to the DEL file
tip: Forensic Delete: when a brand-new (SEQ.NO=1) Record is deleted from the NAU buffer before authorization, the data goes to DEL rather than disappearing. This preserves a forensic audit trail of EVERYTHING that was ever input — even data the user canceled. Required for compliance investigations.

q: When an amendment (SEQ.NO>1) is deleted from NAU, what happens?
options: Physically removed from NAU without writing to DEL
tip: When you delete an AMENDMENT (SEQ.NO>1) draft from NAU, the data is just removed from NAU — nothing goes to DEL. Why? Because the underlying base Record still exists in LIV (and earlier versions in HIS), so there's no need to preserve the deleted amendment separately — the audit chain is already intact.

q: SIM file is used for…
tip: SIM = Simulation file. It holds what-if data completely isolated from live and financial records. Stress tests, what-if loan calculations, scenario analyses all go in SIM. Anything written here is incapable of generating accounting entries or affecting any other lifecycle file.

q: Why is SIM isolated from LIV and FIN data?
options: To prevent simulation from impacting real financial records
tip: Isolation is the point of SIM. Without it, what-if calculations might accidentally affect real customer balances or GL postings. SIM provides a sandbox space inside the same system, governed by the same Tables and rules, but with zero risk of touching real financials.

q: Which file ensures concurrent edit protection?
tip: Edit-locking concurrency control happens through the NAU file. Because NAU is a singleton, only one user can have a draft on a given Record at a time. Any second user trying to edit the same Record gets 'Record Locked'. This protects pending work without needing distributed lock servers.

q: Which file is the immutable, read-only archive?
tip: HIS is the IMMUTABLE, READ-ONLY archive. You can't edit HIS records — they're frozen snapshots. The only way to bring a HIS version back is HISTORY.RESTORE, which copies it into NAU for review/authorization (FIN/CUS) or directly into LIV (INT). This immutability is what makes HIS a trustworthy audit trail.

q: What is the cardinality rule of LIV?
tip: LIV cardinality: strictly ONE record per ID (singleton). Whatever the current authorized state of CUSTOMER 123456 is, that's the single record in LIV. Earlier states live in HIS; pending drafts live in NAU. Singleton means LIV always represents 'the current truth'.

q: What is the cardinality rule of HIS?
tip: HIS cardinality: MANY records per ID. Each prior version gets a sequential suffix (;1, ;2, ;3...). HIS supports unlimited historical versions, capturing the full audit timeline. The highest suffix = the most recently archived version. This is the only lifecycle file that allows multiple records per ID.

q: What is the cardinality rule of NAU?
tip: NAU cardinality: ONE record per ID (singleton). This singleton constraint is critical — it's what enforces edit locking (no two drafts for the same record) and prevents a record from being in HOLD and UNAU statuses simultaneously. Only ONE in-flight change per ID at a time.

q: What is the cardinality rule of DEL?
tip: DEL cardinality: ONE record per ID (singleton). DEL holds deleted brand-new drafts for forensic audit; one deleted draft per record ID. Once the audit period passes (per the bank's retention policy), DEL records may eventually be purged — but during their life, they're singletons.

q: The FILE registry dictates…
options: The valid physical file types for every Table
tip: The FILE Registry is the authoritative configuration that specifies which physical file types (LIV, NAU, HIS, DEL, SIM) each Table uses. A Table's CLASSIFICATION drives the file types — e.g. an INT Table might not need NAU (because it uses 0-Auth direct writes). The Registry makes these choices explicit.

q: What partitioning principle does the FILE registry enforce?
options: Partitioning a single record ID across distinct physical files based on its current state
tip: The FILE Registry partitions a single record ID across distinct physical files based on its CURRENT STATE. For example, CUSTOMER 123456 might exist with state LIVE in LIV, with status UNAU as a draft in NAU, with three prior versions in HIS (;1, ;2, ;3). The Registry coordinates this partitioning.

q: In NAU, the status HOLD means…
tip: HOLD is a record status in NAU. It means: 'unvalidated draft saved by the user'. The user pressed HOLD instead of INPUT because they weren't ready to validate yet — maybe they need to verify something or step away. HOLD records survive crashes (they're persisted in NAU) and can be resumed later.

q: In NAU, status UNAU means…
tip: UNAU is the record status in NAU for a 'completed edit awaiting authorization'. The user pressed INPUT (which ran validation), the data passed, but a second user still has to AUTH it before it can become LIVE. UNAU is the heart of the 4-Eyes Principle.

q: REVE status in HIS means…
tip: REVE is the status in HIS for a record that was REVERSED (canceled after being live). REVE marks the terminal state of a reversed transaction. The full REVERSE flow: LIV → moved to NAU as RNAU → AUTH'd → moved to HIS as REVE. The reversal is now permanent and audit-trail-complete.

q: HNAU stands for…
tip: HNAU stands for 'History Restore Not Authorized'. When you invoke HISTORY.RESTORE on a FIN/CUS Record, the system copies the chosen historical version into NAU with status HNAU — pending a second-user AUTH before it becomes the new LIV record. The H means 'this restoration originated from HIS'.

q: RNAU stands for…
tip: RNAU stands for 'Reversal Not Authorized'. When you REVERSE a LIV Record, it's moved into NAU with status RNAU. A second user must AUTH the reversal, at which point it moves to HIS with status REVE. The R distinguishes a pending reversal from a normal pending edit (UNAU).

q: What does Copy-on-Write mean in HIS context?
options: Old LIV record moves to HIS before the new one becomes active
tip: Copy-on-Write describes the AUTH-time behavior: when an amendment is authorized, the system copies the OLD LIV record into HIS (giving it the next sequential suffix) BEFORE the new amended version replaces it in LIV. This way HIS always retains a complete history without users having to remember to archive.

q: Which file would you query for an authorized customer's last 3 modifications?
tip: If you want to see all of a customer's prior versions, look in HIS — it's the immutable archive of every authorized prior state. Versions are numbered ID;1, ID;2, ID;3 in chronological order of when they were superseded. Current LIV is separate; HIS holds everything that came before.

q: Which file would hold an aborted brand-new record?
tip: A brand-new (SEQ.NO=1) draft that was canceled from NAU is in DEL. DEL exists specifically to capture this case — a Record that was input but never made it to LIV. Without DEL, this canceled data would simply vanish; with DEL, auditors can review every cancellation.

q: What is the role of the NAU file in the 4-eyes principle?
options: Holds the UNAU draft awaiting a second user's approval
tip: NAU's role in the 4-Eyes Principle: the Initiator's INPUT creates a draft in NAU as UNAU (Unauthorized). The Authoriser (a different user) sees the pending UNAU in NAU and AUTH-approves it, moving it to LIV. NAU is the holding area between input and approval — without it, there'd be no place to park 'pending' records.

q: If a record exists in LIV and a draft is created in NAU, which is the latest state shown to the user via projections?
tip: When you ask 'what's the latest state of this record?', the Resolution Waterfall checks NAU FIRST (so pending drafts surface), then LIV (the current authorized state), then HIS (historical versions), then ARC (archive). This priority means users always see the most relevant in-flight state, not stale LIV when a draft exists.

q: Cardinality constraint of NAU exists because…
options: It allows only one record per ID — preventing two states coexisting
tip: NAU's cardinality constraint (one record per ID) is intentional. It serves multiple purposes: prevents two simultaneous drafts, prevents HOLD+UNAU coexistence for the same record, and provides natural edit locking. The single-slot design is a deliberate concurrency-control mechanism.

q: Which space is variable in structure based on use case?
tip: SIM is the lifecycle space whose structure isn't fixed per Table — it can vary based on the simulation use case. Stress tests might need a particular structure; what-if loan calcs might need a different one. SIM gives flexibility because it's isolated from real data — no need for the rigid cardinality of LIV/NAU.

q: In HIS, ID;3 vs ID;1 — which is more recent?
tip: In HIS, ID;3 is MORE RECENT than ID;1. The suffix numbering reflects archival ORDER: each time a LIV record is replaced (by an authorized amendment), the old LIV moves to HIS with the next available suffix. So higher suffix = more recently archived = more recent prior state.

q: Why does DEL exist?
options: Forensic audit trail of canceled new records
tip: DEL exists for forensic audit. When a brand-new (SEQ.NO=1) draft Record is deleted before being authorized, DEL preserves it. This way, regulators can verify the FULL history of what was input — including data that was input then canceled. Without DEL, evidence of cancellations would be lost forever.

q: True or False: A record can be in LIV and NAU at the same time (with different statuses).
options: True — LIV is authorized live, NAU holds the pending draft
False — only one file at a time
tip: Yes — a Record can be in LIV (as the current authorized state) and in NAU (as a pending edit or HOLD draft) at the same time. They're DIFFERENT lifecycle files holding the SAME ID but different states. The Resolution Waterfall in Projections surfaces the most relevant state (NAU first if it exists, else LIV).

q: NAU's edit locking prevents…
options: Concurrent modification conflicts on the same LIV record
tip: NAU's edit locking is what prevents two users from concurrently editing the same LIV record without conflict. Because NAU is a singleton, the first user's draft occupies the slot; the second user can't start a competing draft until the first is resolved (AUTH'd, DELETE'd, or aged out). No distributed lock manager needed.

q: Lifecycle spaces ensure…
options: Strict data integrity and lifecycle consistency
tip: Distinct lifecycle spaces (LIV, NAU, HIS, DEL, SIM) with strict cardinality rules (singletons for LIV/NAU/DEL; many-per-ID for HIS; flexible for SIM) ensure data integrity. Each space has a specific role; together they enforce immutability of history, isolation of drafts, edit locking, and forensic capture of cancellations.

q: Which operation retrieves and displays a specific record?
tip: SEE is the read operation — it retrieves and displays a Record. It uses the Resolution Waterfall to find the most relevant version: NAU first (so pending drafts surface), then LIV (current authorized), then HIS (historical), then ARC (deep archive). If no version exists anywhere, it returns 'Record not found'.

q: In what order does SEE perform the Resolution Waterfall?
tip: SEE's Resolution Waterfall order: NAU (Unauthorized — pending drafts) → LIV (Live — current authorized) → HIS (History — archived versions) → ARC (deep Archive). The reason NAU comes first: if the user has an in-flight draft, they should see THAT, not the stale LIV. This priority is built into the read path.

q: What does SEE return if no record exists in any space?
tip: 'Record not found' is returned by SEE only when ALL four lifecycle layers (NAU, LIV, HIS, ARC) are empty for the given ID. The record genuinely doesn't exist anywhere. This is distinct from 'Record Locked' (which means a NAU draft exists owned by someone else).

q: What does INPUT do for FIN/CUS classification?
options: Runs validations and creates a record in NAU with status UNAU
tip: INPUT on FIN/CUS Tables runs validation, then writes the Record to NAU with status UNAU (Unauthorized). The record is NOT yet live — it sits in NAU awaiting a second user's AUTH approval. This is the standard 4-Eyes flow for sensitive transactional data.

q: What does INPUT do for INT classification with 0-Auth configured?
options: Skips NAU and writes directly to LIV
tip: INPUT on INT Tables behaves differently if the Table is configured for 0-Auth: validation still runs, but the record is written DIRECTLY to LIV (bypassing NAU). The old LIV record is moved to HIS at the same time. This is reserved for low-risk installation parameters (like CURRENCY) where 4-Eyes adds friction without security benefit.

q: HOLD operation creates a record in…
tip: HOLD saves an UNVALIDATED draft into NAU with status HOLD. Unlike INPUT, no validation runs — the user can save partial/incomplete work and resume later. The draft persists across sessions (it's in NAU on disk), so a crash won't lose it. HOLD = 'I'm not done, save and let me come back'.

q: Does HOLD require validation?
options: No — it saves drafts without validation
tip: HOLD intentionally SKIPS validation. The whole point of HOLD is to let users save in-progress work without needing all fields to be valid yet. INPUT runs validation; HOLD doesn't. When the user returns and presses INPUT, validation then runs against the completed data.

q: Why does the system block HOLD if a UNAU record already exists for the same ID?
options: NAU only allows one record per ID — writing a draft would overwrite the pending committed record
tip: If a record with UNAU status already exists in NAU (a completed edit awaiting authorization), HOLD on the same ID is BLOCKED. Why? Because NAU is a singleton (one record per ID). If HOLD wrote a draft on top of the UNAU, the pending committed work would be lost. The system protects it by refusing the HOLD.

q: VALIDATE operation does what?
options: Evaluates business logic without saving to the database
tip: VALIDATE runs the business rules against a payload but does NOT save anything. It returns Success or Error. The use case: front-end forms can VALIDATE-check the data as the user fills it in, giving instant feedback without persisting half-baked data to NAU.

q: VALIDATE is commonly used by…
options: Front-end clients to verify before submission
tip: Front-end clients use VALIDATE to verify data before final submission. The user fills the form; the client calls VALIDATE; the server returns Success or Error; the user fixes any issues; finally the client calls INPUT to commit. VALIDATE is the 'dry run' that's perfect for instant UX feedback.

q: AUTH executes which principle?
tip: AUTH (authorize) implements the 4-Eyes Principle: it requires a DIFFERENT user (the Authoriser) to approve a record that another user (the Initiator) created. AUTH moves a NAU record (UNAU, HNAU, or RNAU statuses) into LIV (or for reversals, into HIS as REVE).

q: During AUTH on a UNAU record, what happens?
options: UNAU moves to LIV and prior LIV is archived to HIS
tip: When AUTH is called on a UNAU record in NAU: (1) the record moves from NAU to LIV with status LIVE; (2) the previous LIV record (if any) is copy-on-write archived into HIS with the next sequential suffix. So one AUTH triggers two file moves, maintaining the audit chain.

q: AUTH also handles which special status reversal?
tip: AUTH handles three cases: (1) UNAU → LIV (standard authorization), (2) HNAU → LIV (history restore authorization), and (3) RNAU → HIS as REVE (reversal authorization). In all three cases, AUTH is the gateway between 'pending' and 'committed'.

q: REVERSE on FIN/CUS does what?
options: Moves LIV to NAU with status RNAU
tip: REVERSE on a FIN/CUS Table doesn't immediately undo the transaction — it creates an RNAU (Reversal Not Authorized) entry in NAU. A second user must AUTH the reversal. Only after AUTH does the reversal complete, moving the record to HIS with status REVE. This applies 4-Eyes to reversals just like to inputs.

q: REVERSE on INT does what?
options: Directly moves LIV to HIS with status REVE
tip: REVERSE on an INT Table (especially 0-Auth ones) is simpler: it directly moves the LIV record to HIS with status REVE, no second user required. This applies to low-risk installation parameters where requiring two users to undo would add friction without security benefit.

q: DELETE on a brand-new record (SEQ.NO=1) does what?
tip: DELETE on a brand-new (SEQ.NO=1) NAU draft: the record moves from NAU to DEL. This preserves a forensic audit trail of the canceled draft. So even though the user 'deleted' the record, evidence of its existence is retained for compliance review.

q: DELETE on an amendment (SEQ.NO>1) does what?
options: Physically deletes from NAU without writing to DEL
tip: DELETE on an AMENDMENT (SEQ.NO>1) NAU draft: the record is simply removed from NAU — NOTHING is written to DEL. Why? Because the underlying base record still exists in LIV (and earlier versions in HIS), so the audit chain is already intact. Deleting an amendment is treated as a 'cancel amendment' action.

q: HISTORY.RESTORE on FIN/CUS creates a…
options: HNAU copy in NAU buffer for review and authorization
tip: HISTORY.RESTORE on FIN/CUS Tables creates a HNAU (History Restore Not Authorized) entry in NAU. A second user must then AUTH the restoration before the historical version becomes the new LIV record. This applies 4-Eyes to historical restores — you can't sneak a past state back into production without approval.

q: HISTORY.RESTORE on INT does what?
tip: HISTORY.RESTORE on INT Tables (especially 0-Auth ones) can directly overwrite the LIV record with the chosen historical version. The current LIV moves to HIS for audit purposes. This is allowed because INT parameters are typically low-risk.

q: 0-Auth exception applies to which kind of table?
tip: 0-Auth is a Table-level configuration applied to specific INT (Installation) Tables like CURRENCY. It bypasses the NAU buffer entirely — INPUT writes directly to LIV (with the old LIV moving to HIS for audit). The 4-Eyes Principle is skipped for these low-risk global parameters.

q: With 0-Auth, when a user commits a change, what happens to the old LIV record?
options: Written to HIS, new input written directly to LIV
tip: Under 0-Auth, when a user commits a change: the old LIV record is written to HIS (preserving audit), and the new input is written DIRECTLY to LIV (becoming the active version). NAU is bypassed. This works because INT parameters change rarely and aren't sensitive financial data.

q: Direct database modifications are…
options: Prohibited — all interactions must use standard operations
tip: Direct database modifications (SQL UPDATE on the underlying tables) are STRICTLY PROHIBITED in CBS. All interactions must go through the standard operations (SEE, INPUT, AUTH, etc.) which enforce validation, authorization, audit, and lifecycle rules. Bypassing them would defeat every control the system provides.

q: Which operations are part of the standard list?
options: SEE, INPUT, HOLD, VALIDATE, AUTH, REVERSE, DELETE, HISTORY.RESTORE
tip: The eight standard operations are: SEE (read), INPUT (create/edit), HOLD (save draft), VALIDATE (dry-run rules), AUTH (approve), REVERSE (cancel a LIV record), DELETE (cancel a NAU draft), HISTORY.RESTORE (reinstate a past version). These are the ONLY ways to interact with Records — direct DB modification is forbidden.

q: Why does FIN/CUS INPUT route through NAU instead of LIV?
options: To enforce the Four-Eyes Principle / authorization
tip: FIN/CUS INPUT routes through NAU specifically to enforce the 4-Eyes Principle. The Initiator's input goes to NAU as UNAU; a different user must AUTH it before it affects LIV (and the GL). Without NAU as a buffer, there'd be no place to park 'pending' records awaiting approval.

q: INT tables that do not use 0-Auth would behave like…
tip: Without 0-Auth configured, an INT Table's INPUT behaves like FIN/CUS — routing through NAU as UNAU, requiring a second user's AUTH. Tables with 0-Auth (like CURRENCY) are explicitly configured to skip NAU. 0-Auth is opt-in, not default.

q: What does INAU mean in the context of projections?
tip: INAU is a flag/status used in Projections and Enquiries to indicate that an authorized LIV record has a PENDING unauthorized draft in NAU. So when Enquiries do Virtual Data Merging, they show the row with an INAU marker to alert users that an unauthorized change is in-flight. It's the visible signal of a pending edit.

q: REVE status indicates…
tip: REVE is the terminal status in HIS for a reversed transaction. When you REVERSE a LIV record and a second user AUTHs the reversal, the record moves from NAU (as RNAU) to HIS with status REVE. REVE means 'this transaction was reversed/canceled and is archived'.

q: After AUTH on a UNAU, the new RECORD.STATUS becomes…
tip: After AUTH on an UNAU record, the record's status becomes LIVE — it now sits in LIV as the active version. Concurrently, the previous LIV version is archived to HIS with the next suffix. The status transition (UNAU → LIVE) and the file move (NAU → LIV) happen atomically.

q: A user is editing CUSTOMER record CUS123. Someone else has UNAU in NAU. The system returns…
tip: When Alice has CUS123 in NAU as UNAU and Bob tries to edit, the system returns 'Record Locked'. This is because NAU is a SINGLETON (one record per ID) — Bob can't create a competing draft. The lock protects Alice's pending work from being overwritten. Concurrency control built into the file structure.

q: Which operation buffers data for the 4-eyes flow?
tip: INPUT on FIN/CUS buffers data into NAU as UNAU specifically to enable the 4-Eyes flow. The Initiator's INPUT puts a record in NAU; a different user's AUTH moves it to LIV. NAU is the buffer that makes 4-Eyes possible — without it, INPUT would have to write directly to LIV or fail.

q: Why does VALIDATE not write to the DB?
options: To prevent writes during inspection — it is a check-only operation
tip: VALIDATE doesn't write because it's a CHECK-ONLY operation. The use case: front-end forms call VALIDATE to verify data quality before submission, getting instant Success/Error without persisting half-baked records to NAU. If we wrote during VALIDATE, every form interaction would clutter NAU with discarded drafts.

q: AUTH on HNAU triggers…
options: Restore overwrite into LIV with HIS archival
tip: AUTH on an HNAU (History Restore Not Authorized): the system moves the restored historical version from NAU to LIV with status LIVE, and the prior LIV is archived to HIS. Effectively: the restored past state becomes the new present, and the state that WAS present joins history.

q: Which standard operation requires no second user under 0-Auth?
options: All of the above on INT 0-Auth
tip: Under 0-Auth (INT classification), INPUT, REVERSE, and HISTORY.RESTORE all bypass the requirement for a second user. The operation completes immediately, with old LIV → HIS and new state → LIV. This is reserved for low-risk installation parameters; FIN/CUS Tables never bypass 4-Eyes.

q: In the standard ops, REVERSE differs from DELETE because…
options: REVERSE cancels a LIVE transaction; DELETE removes an unauthorized draft
DELETE works on LIV, REVERSE on NAU
tip: REVERSE cancels a LIVE transaction (something already in LIV). DELETE cancels an unauthorized draft (something still in NAU). They operate on different lifecycle stages: REVERSE creates an RNAU in NAU pending AUTH; DELETE removes a NAU draft (forensically to DEL if SEQ.NO=1, otherwise just removed).

q: After DELETE on a SEQ.NO=1 record, the record physically ends up in…
tip: After DELETE on a SEQ.NO=1 (brand new) Record: the data moves from NAU to DEL. DEL is the singleton file specifically for canceled brand-new drafts — preserving forensic evidence that the record was input then canceled before going live.

q: After DELETE on a SEQ.NO>1 record, the data ends up…
options: Removed from NAU; base remains in LIV/HIS
tip: After DELETE on a SEQ.NO>1 (amendment) Record: the draft is simply removed from NAU. Nothing goes to DEL because the audit chain is already intact — the base record still lives in LIV and earlier versions in HIS. Treating amendment deletions as 'cancel amendment' keeps DEL focused only on irretrievable cases.

q: INPUT on INT (non-0-Auth) writes to…
tip: INPUT on an INT Table that is NOT configured for 0-Auth still routes through NAU as UNAU — exactly like FIN/CUS. The 0-Auth bypass is opt-in; without it, INT INPUT requires authorization just like everything else. 0-Auth is set per Table in the FILE Registry.

q: What is the practical purpose of HOLD?
tip: HOLD's practical purpose: a teller starts entering a complex transaction (e.g. a 20-line trade), gets interrupted, needs to step away. She presses HOLD — the partial work is saved to NAU as HOLD, unvalidated, no second-user approval pending. Later she returns and continues. INPUT validates and moves it to UNAU; AUTH moves it live.

q: What does AUTH ensure regarding the prior LIV record?
tip: AUTH archives the prior LIV record to HIS as part of the atomic transition. The pre-amendment LIV (whatever was live before) gets the next sequential suffix in HIS (;n). This Copy-on-Write happens automatically — admins don't have to remember to archive. Without it, you'd lose history every time a record was amended.

q: Can DELETE remove a LIVE record directly?
options: No — DELETE works on NAU drafts only; LIV must be REVERSEd
tip: DELETE works ONLY on NAU drafts. It cannot remove a LIVE record — for that, you must use REVERSE. The reason: a live record may be referenced by the GL, by other transactions, by reports; you can't just nuke it. REVERSE creates a controlled, audited cancellation. DELETE is for canceling work that never made it to LIV.

q: Resolution Waterfall benefits which standard operation primarily?
tip: The Resolution Waterfall is most critical for SEE — the read operation. SEE checks NAU → LIV → HIS → ARC, returning the FIRST hit. This means SEE always surfaces the most relevant state (pending drafts beat live; live beats history; history beats archive).

q: After REVERSE on FIN/CUS, the LIV record is now in…
tip: After REVERSE on a FIN/CUS record: the record is in NAU with status RNAU (Reversal Not Authorized). LIV is now empty for that ID (until the reversal is AUTH'd). A second user reviews and AUTHs the reversal, after which it moves to HIS with status REVE.

q: After AUTH on an RNAU, the record ends up in…
tip: After AUTH on an RNAU record: the record moves from NAU to HIS with status REVE. The reversal is now final and archived. LIV has no current authorized version for that ID. If someone tries to SEE the record, they'll get HIS data (or a 'no current state' indication depending on the projection).

q: What does ARC represent in the Resolution Waterfall?
options: Archive — the deepest layer of historical storage
tip: ARC is the deepest layer in the Resolution Waterfall — a long-term archive that sits below HIS. SEE checks NAU → LIV → HIS → ARC in that order. Most reads will resolve in NAU, LIV, or HIS; ARC is the safety net for very old records that have been migrated out of active HIS.

q: True or False: All INT tables automatically use 0-Auth.
options: False — only tables specifically configured for 0-Auth
tip: Not all INT Tables use 0-Auth — it's a specific opt-in configuration on the FILE Registry. Some INT Tables (e.g. CURRENCY) are configured for 0-Auth; others remain 4-Eyes like FIN/CUS. The configuration is per Table, based on the bank's risk assessment of that Table's content.

q: INPUT validation runs at which moment for FIN/CUS?
options: Immediately on input, before saving to NAU
tip: On INPUT for FIN/CUS, validation runs IMMEDIATELY before the record is written to NAU. The rules are checked synchronously; if any fail, the record is NOT created and the user gets an error. Only if validation passes does the record land in NAU as UNAU. This ensures NAU never contains invalid data (unlike HOLD which intentionally skips validation).

q: In HOLD, validation runs?
tip: HOLD intentionally SKIPS validation because its purpose is to save IN-PROGRESS, possibly-incomplete drafts. Forcing validation on HOLD would defeat the purpose — the user couldn't save partial work to resume later. HOLD = save and resume; INPUT = validate and commit.

q: What is the practical purpose of HISTORY.RESTORE?
options: Reinstate a past version of a record from HIS
tip: HISTORY.RESTORE retrieves a specific historical version (from HIS) and reinstates it as the current Record. For FIN/CUS Tables, this goes through HNAU (History Restore Not Authorized) in NAU pending second-user AUTH. For 0-Auth INT Tables, it can overwrite LIV directly. Useful for reverting an amendment.

q: Which operation can both authorize forward edits and finalize reversals?
tip: AUTH handles both forward edits (UNAU → LIV, with old LIV → HIS) AND reversals (RNAU → HIS as REVE) AND history restores (HNAU → LIV, with old LIV → HIS). It's the single 'approve' operation that finalizes any pending NAU state, regardless of its specific status.

q: The Resolution Waterfall returns 'Record not found' when…
options: Record exists nowhere across NAU, LIV, HIS, ARC
tip: 'Record not found' is returned by SEE only when the record exists in NONE of NAU, LIV, HIS, or ARC. The full Resolution Waterfall has been exhausted with no hit. This is genuinely 'the record doesn't exist anywhere' — distinct from 'Record Locked' (where it exists but is owned by another draft in NAU).

q: What is the DDD (Dynamic Data Dictionary)?
options: A metadata-driven abstraction layer decoupling physical storage from logical view
tip: The Dynamic Data Dictionary (DDD) is the metadata layer that defines the SHAPE of every Table — what Fields exist, what types they are, what validation rules apply. Crucially, it DECOUPLES the logical business view (e.g. 'CUSTOMER.NAME') from physical storage (e.g. 'field 1, position 5'). This means schema changes don't require database recompilation or kernel changes.

q: DDD eliminates the need for what?
tip: Without the DDD, every schema change would require Data Definition Language (DDL) changes to the physical database, plus potentially kernel recompilation — slow, risky, and disruptive. The DDD eliminates this by treating schema as METADATA: change the dictionary, and the new structure applies immediately without touching the database engine.

q: DDD definitions cannot function unless the target table is…
options: Officially registered in the FILE registry
tip: A DDD dictionary definition (in TABLE.DEF and FIELD.DEF records) cannot work unless its target Table is officially registered in the FILE Registry. The FILE Registry is the prerequisite — it tells the system the Table exists and is governed. Only then does the DDD layer on field definitions on top.

q: Which DDD records are commonly referenced?
tip: The DDD lives in two key records: TABLE.DEF (one per Table — defines its overall structure, classification reference) and FIELD.DEF (one per field — defines its name, type, validation rule, attributes). These two records form the complete metadata model for a Table's data layout.

q: DDD relies on which classification to decide global vs company-specific?
tip: DDD's behavior depends on the Table's FILE classification (INT/FIN/CUS). For INT Tables (installation-wide), field definitions apply globally across every Company. For FIN/CUS (per-company segregated), field definitions can be company-specific. The classification, set in the FILE Registry, determines DDD's scope.

q: DDD maps logical field names to…
tip: The DDD maps LOGICAL field names (e.g. CUSTOMER.NAME) to PHYSICAL data array positions (e.g. Field 1, Multi-value position 1). Code never references the physical position — it uses the logical name. The DDD does the translation. This is why renaming or reordering fields doesn't require code changes.

q: System Type 'D' (Data) means…
options: Physical data stored directly in the database
tip: D (Data) is the simplest System Type — a field that holds actual data stored in the physical database. Most fields are Type D. Examples: CUSTOMER.SHORT.NAME, ACCOUNT.WORKING.BALANCE. Type D fields persist; their values are written to and read from disk.

q: System Type 'I' (I-Descriptor) means…
options: Logical descriptor calculated at runtime, never written to DB
tip: I (I-Descriptor / Virtual) fields are COMPUTED IN MEMORY at runtime — never persisted to disk. They're derived from other fields. Example: FULL.NAME = FIRST.NAME + ' ' + LAST.NAME. When you read FULL.NAME, the DDD computes it on-the-fly. No risk of stale derived data; no storage cost.

q: System Type 'J' (Join) means…
options: Dynamic link fetching data from related tables (foreign key)
tip: J (Join) fields fetch their value DYNAMICALLY from a related Table via a foreign-key relationship at display time. Example: on an ACCOUNT screen, the customer's name is a J field — at display, the system reads ACCOUNT.CUSTOMER (a Type D field holding the customer ID), then joins to the CUSTOMER Table to pull the name. No physical FK required.

q: Concatenating First+Last Name on the fly is an example of…
tip: FULL.NAME as 'FIRST + LAST' is the textbook I-Descriptor (Type I) example. The value is COMPUTED at runtime by concatenating two stored fields, then displayed. It's never persisted, so there's no risk of FIRST changing and FULL.NAME going stale. The Field declaration in DDD specifies the computation logic.

q: Displaying a customer's name on an account record by foreign key uses…
tip: Showing the customer's name on an ACCOUNT record uses a J (Join) field. The ACCOUNT Record stores ACCOUNT.CUSTOMER (the customer's ID) as Type D. The customer's NAME is a Type J on the ACCOUNT projection — at display, the system fetches it from the CUSTOMER Table via the ID. No physical foreign key needed at the DB level.

q: Non-1NF support means…
options: A single record can natively store multi-dimensional arrays and repeating blocks
tip: Non-1NF (Non-First Normal Form) means a single Record can natively store MULTI-DIMENSIONAL arrays and repeating blocks (like a loan's repayment schedule). Standard 1NF databases would require a separate child table for the repayments; CBS's DDD lets you store them all inside the parent record — no joins needed.

q: Field Associations (Blocks) use which markers?
tip: Blocks use < for Start, - for Member, and > for End markers.

q: If a new index is added inside an Association, what happens?
options: System automatically expands or shifts associated fields to maintain index alignment
tip: Field Associations ensure index alignment within multi-value blocks. If you add a new entry at index 3 of a Repayment Schedule, ALL associated fields (date, amount, balance, etc.) automatically shift to make room — keeping their indices aligned. Without this, the values for one entry could drift away from each other.

q: The Hot-Swappable Validation Engine means…
options: The validations only run at restart
Validation rules can change without code recompilation/restart
tip: Hot-Swappable Validation Engine: the DDD binds each field type to pre-compiled validation routines (validateDate, validateAmount, validateCurrency, etc.). Changing a field's TYPE in the dictionary (e.g. Alphanumeric → Numeric) immediately applies the new rule — no system restart, no kernel recompile. The next input is validated by the new rule.

q: Field.type links fields to…
tip: Field.type in the DDD is the attribute that links a field to its validation routine. So Field.type='Numeric' connects to validateAmount; Field.type='Date' connects to validateDate. These routines are pre-compiled and registered; changing a field's type just swaps which routine is invoked. Hot-swap means immediate effect.

q: To avoid creating thousands of tiny reference tables, DDD integrates with…
tip: VIRTUAL.TABLES is the system's centralized reference-list storage. Instead of creating thousands of tiny static reference Tables (Risk Levels, Statuses, Categories), all such lists live in a single Table called VIRTUAL.TABLES — distinguished by compound keys (e.g. RISK.LEVEL|HIGH, STATUS|ACTIVE). DDD links fields to these entries for dropdowns.

q: Static reference lists are stored in…
tip: Reference lists in VIRTUAL.TABLES use COMPOUND KEYS to distinguish entries — for example, RISK.LEVEL|HIGH, RISK.LEVEL|MEDIUM, RISK.LEVEL|LOW for one list; STATUS|ACTIVE, STATUS|INACTIVE for another. The first part of the key is the list name; the second is the entry. One physical Table, many logical lists.

q: When a dictionary field links to virtual tables, the system…
options: Auto-generates UI dropdowns and validates inputs against records
tip: When a DDD field is linked to entries in VIRTUAL.TABLES, the system AUTO-GENERATES the UI dropdown (showing the entries as options) and validates input against the entries (rejecting values not in the list). No code needed — the dropdown and validation flow from the DDD-to-virtual-tables link.

q: How does DDD support custom local fields added by a bank?
options: System automatically injects user-defined fields into TABLE.DEF
tip: Custom local fields added by the bank are automatically INJECTED into TABLE.DEF via the DDD's customization framework. The system extends the Table to include the new field, and it's available everywhere (projections, enquiries, validations) immediately — without altering vendor code or recompiling.

q: True or False: DDD requires changing physical storage when adding new fields.
options: False — abstraction allows new fields without altering physical structure
tip: DDD's abstraction means new fields are METADATA additions, not physical schema migrations. Existing physical Records on disk remain untouched; the new field is simply a new logical position with no value yet. No migration, no downtime. This is the killer feature that makes adding fields trivial.

q: DDD's primary benefit is…
options: Decoupling logical schema from physical storage
tip: DDD's primary benefit: decoupling the LOGICAL business view (fields like CUSTOMER.NAME, validation rules) from PHYSICAL storage (where data sits on disk). Schema evolves through metadata; the database doesn't need DDL changes. This is what makes CBS configurable without code releases.

q: Which is NOT a System Type in DDD?
tip: The three System Types are D (Data — stored on disk), I (I-Descriptor — computed at runtime, never stored), and J (Join — fetched from related table at display time). Z is not a defined type. Together, D/I/J cover storage, computation, and relational fetch — the three ways data can 'be on a record'.

q: An admin changes a field from Alphanumeric to Numeric. The change applies…
tip: Validation rule changes (e.g. Alphanumeric → Numeric) apply IMMEDIATELY after the DDD update — no kernel recompile, no restart. This is the Hot-Swappable Validation Engine in action. The next user input is validated by the new rule; the old data on disk is unchanged but new inputs must comply.

q: What is the role of < and > markers in DDD?
options: Defining Start and End of a Field Association block
tip: Field Association markers in the DDD: < marks the START of an association block, - marks each MEMBER field within the block, > marks the END. Within a block, all multi-value fields are kept index-aligned. So adding an entry shifts all members consistently — preserving the relationship between, say, date[3], amount[3], balance[3].

q: Why does DDD allow non-1NF?
options: To natively store multi-dim arrays/repeating blocks without child tables
tip: Non-1NF support exists so a SINGLE Record can store repeating blocks (Repayment Schedule, multiple phone numbers, multiple counterparties) without requiring separate child Tables. This is a deliberate departure from 1NF that simplifies data modeling for banking domain objects — keeping related data co-located.

q: Multi-valued INPUTTER is enabled by which DDD feature?
tip: Multi-valued INPUTTER (and AUTHORISER) in the audit block is enabled by DDD's Non-1NF support — a single Record stores an array of inputter user IDs (one per modification cycle) inside the audit Fields without needing a child table. Each edit cycle adds a new entry to the array.

q: Which fields are never persisted to the physical database?
tip: I-Descriptor (Type I) fields are NEVER persisted to disk. They're computed in memory at runtime each time the record is read. This avoids stale derived data — if the underlying source fields change, the computed value automatically reflects the new inputs on the next read. Trade-off: tiny CPU cost per read.

q: Joined fields (J) avoid which traditional approach?
tip: J (Join) fields avoid PHYSICAL foreign keys at the database layer. Traditional RDBMS would require an actual FK constraint linking ACCOUNT.CUSTOMER to CUSTOMER. With J fields, the DDD declares the link as metadata and resolves it dynamically at display time. Cleaner schema; less DDL.

q: Which artifact registers a Table's fields and rules?
tip: TABLE.DEF and FIELD.DEF are the DDD's two key records. TABLE.DEF describes a Table's overall structure (which fields it has, in what order, classification reference). FIELD.DEF describes EACH field's specifics (name, type, validation routine, mandatory/optional, multi-value rules). Together, they define everything about a Table's data layout.

q: A repeating Repayment Schedule is best modeled via…
options: Non-1NF associated multi-value fields in DDD
tip: A Repayment Schedule (date, amount, balance, ... for each installment) is best modeled as Non-1NF associated multi-value fields in the DDD. All installments live inside the parent loan Record — no child table required. Field Associations keep date[i], amount[i], balance[i] index-aligned via < - > markers.

q: DDD validation rules are enforced at which layer?
tip: DDD enforces data integrity at the TABLE DEFINITION (application) layer — not at the physical DB layer. So even if you used a different underlying DB engine, the validation rules would still apply because they live in the DDD metadata. This makes the system more portable and the rules more explicit.

q: VIRTUAL.TABLES uses what to distinguish entries?
tip: VIRTUAL.TABLES uses compound keys to distinguish many logical reference lists inside one physical Table. The key format is LIST_NAME|ENTRY (e.g. STATUS|ACTIVE). The first part identifies the list; the second is the specific entry. Lookup just queries by compound key.

q: Custom local fields show up in the application…
tip: Custom local fields appear in the application IMMEDIATELY after DDD injection — no redeployment needed. The bank adds the field to TABLE.DEF; the next time any projection/enquiry/validation reads the dictionary, it picks up the new field. No code, no compile, no restart.

q: Which is true about DDD's relationship with FILE classification?
options: DDD relies on it to determine global vs company scope
tip: DDD relies on FILE classification (INT/FIN/CUS) to decide field-definition scope. INT classification → field definitions apply globally (every Company sees the same fields). FIN/CUS classification → field definitions can vary per Company (each Company may have local customizations). This is how DDD respects multi-company segregation.

q: What is a Projection?
options: A configuration for a Table defining display, entry, validation, processing
tip: A Projection is the configurable LENS over a Table — it defines how data is displayed to users, what they can enter, what gets validated, and how the record is processed (including authorization rules). One Table can have many Projections (e.g. CUSTOMER, CUSTOMER,CORP, CUSTOMER~TELLER) — each tailored for a different role or workflow.

q: Projections decouple…
options: UI/transaction behavior from physical database schema
tip: Projections decouple the USER INTERFACE and transaction behavior from the PHYSICAL DATABASE schema. Edit the Projection, and the UI/workflow changes — without touching the underlying Table structure. This is what makes the system Server-Driven UI (SDUI) capable.

q: What operations always go through Projections?
tip: All user-facing operations (Input, Authorize, Reverse, See) must go through Projections — they are the mandatory gateway between users and the database. Direct DB writes are forbidden; every mutation passes through a registered PROJECTION.DEF, ensuring rules, audit, and authorization are enforced consistently.

q: What does Server-Driven UI (SDUI) mean?
options: Backend returns composite JSON schema for layout, fields, validations
tip: Server-Driven UI (SDUI) means the BACKEND returns the form layout/field definitions as JSON when a user requests a Projection. The frontend doesn't hardcode forms — it renders whatever the server sends. Edit the Projection in config; clients see the new layout on next request. No client redeployment.

q: Resolution Waterfall in Projections checks files in which order?
tip: Projections use the same Resolution Waterfall as SEE — they check NAU (Unauthorized) first (so pending drafts surface), then LIV (Live — current authorized), then HIS (History), then ARC (Archive). This ensures users always see the most relevant in-flight state when opening a record.

q: What does the Diff Engine do?
options: Highlights visual differences between draft vs live or two HIS versions
tip: The Diff Engine in Projections highlights VISUAL DIFFERENCES between a draft and the live baseline — at AUTH time it shows the Authoriser exactly what changed. It can also compare two historical versions for audit purposes. This makes 4-Eyes review fast and accurate — the Authoriser doesn't have to compare 200 fields manually.

q: A simplified CUSTOMER~SIMPLE projection shows…
tip: A CUSTOMER~SIMPLE Projection might show only 5 fields a teller needs — Name, Account, Balance, Phone, Notes — instead of the 200+ fields on the full CUSTOMER Table. This 'Targeted Subset View' approach reduces clutter, accelerates training, and prevents tellers from accidentally editing fields they shouldn't.

q: If a projection ID matches the table name exactly (e.g. ACCOUNT), it…
options: Acts as the Master View rendering all default fields
tip: If a Projection's ID exactly matches the Table name (e.g. ACCOUNT — no version suffix), it acts as the MASTER VIEW — rendering all default fields. Variants like ACCOUNT,CORP or ACCOUNT~TELLER are role-specific subsets. The Master View is the fallback when no role-specific Projection is invoked.

q: NOINPUT field property means…
tip: NOINPUT is a Field Property Override in a Projection: it makes the field READ-ONLY in this specific Projection. The user can SEE the field but can't edit it. The underlying DDD definition is unchanged — other Projections on the same Table can still write to it.

q: NOCHANGE means…
tip: NOCHANGE makes a field IMMUTABLE AFTER CREATION. You can set it ONCE when creating the record; after that, no one can change it through this Projection. Useful for fields like Customer ID, Account Opening Date, or any 'set at birth' attribute that shouldn't drift over time.

q: HIDDEN means…
options: Used for background logic but removed from the UI
tip: HIDDEN means the field is used in BACKGROUND LOGIC (defaulting, validation, plugins) but is REMOVED from the user-visible UI. The user doesn't see it; the system still uses it. Useful for technical fields like internal flags that drive behavior without needing user input.

q: Field Property Overrides can do which of the following?
options: Make an optional field MANDATORY in a specific projection
tip: Field Property Overrides in a Projection let you constrain fields per role/scenario: make an optional DDD field MANDATORY in this Projection; make it NOINPUT (read-only); make it NOCHANGE (immutable after creation); make it HIDDEN (invisible). One Table, many Projections — each with its own constraints.

q: Composite Layouts in projections support…
tip: Composite Layouts in Projections support modern UX: collapsible SECTIONS/CLUSTERS (group related fields together with a header), Associated Projections that split big tasks into TABS (e.g. General / Interest / Collateral on a mortgage form), and tooltips. This avoids overwhelming users with hundreds of flat fields.

q: What is the purpose of Associated Projections?
options: Split massive products (e.g. mortgages) into navigable logical Tabs
tip: Associated Projections split a massive product (like a mortgage with 200 fields) into navigable logical TABS — General, Interest, Collateral, Documents, etc. Each tab is its own Associated Projection. Users move through them in sequence or as needed. Much easier than one giant scrolling form.

q: Suppress See Mode does what?
options: Hides non-participating attributes from the payload
tip: Suppress See Mode is a Projection setting that mathematically HIDES all non-participating or non-editable attributes from the payload — keeping the UI focused. So if a Projection is configured to view only certain fields, the irrelevant ones are stripped from the response entirely. Reduces clutter and load size.

q: Automated Defaulting can use which sources?
options: Fixed values, linked records, system vars (TODAY, USER, COMPANY)
tip: Automated Defaulting in Projections pre-fills field values without user typing. Sources: FIXED values (e.g. country=PT), LINKED records (fetch a value from a related Table when an ID is entered), or SYSTEM VARIABLES like TODAY (current bank date), USER (current user ID), COMPANY (current company). Saves time and reduces errors.

q: What are Hot Fields?
options: ON.CHANGE fields that trigger validation/defaulting on tab-out
tip: Hot Fields trigger logic when the user changes a field and tabs out (the onBlur event). The associated plugin runs immediately — for validation (e.g. check ID against a registry) or auto-population (e.g. pull the customer's name when their ID is entered). Instant feedback before form submission.

q: Pre/Post hooks in projections are written in…
tip: Pre/Post hooks in Projections execute custom JAVA or GROOVY scripts — fragments of code attached to specific lifecycle events. Pre-hooks run BEFORE the operation commits (validation, external API calls); post-hooks run AFTER successful commit (notifications, cascading updates). The scripts are registered as Plugins.

q: The AUTH.REQUIRED flag does what?
options: If YES, saves data as unauthorized draft (INAU), enforcing 4-eyes
tip: AUTH.REQUIRED is a Projection flag. When YES, INPUT writes the record to NAU as UNAU (Unauthorized) — requiring a second user's AUTH approval before it becomes LIVE. This is what implements the 4-Eyes Principle at the Projection level. Setting NO bypasses 4-Eyes (use sparingly for low-risk operations).

q: What does INAU mean?
tip: INAU is the visible MARKER that a LIV record has a pending UNAU change in NAU — visible in Enquiries and on Projections. So users see 'this record is live, but a draft amendment is awaiting authorization'. The Resolution Waterfall and Virtual Data Merging surface this flag automatically.

q: Projections forbid direct API writes — why?
options: To enforce business rules and the 4-eyes principle
tip: Projections FORBID direct API writes to the database. All writes pass through a registered PROJECTION.DEF, ensuring rules (validation, plugins, AUTH.REQUIRED, field overrides) are enforced. This is a foundational architectural rule — bypassing it would defeat every control the system provides.

q: What is the practical example of CUSTOMER,CORP projection?
options: A version of the CUSTOMER projection for corporate clients
tip: CUSTOMER,CORP is a comma-suffix Projection (or 'version') of the CUSTOMER Table — typically a corporate-customer variant showing fields relevant to companies (TAX.ID, INCORPORATION.DATE, DIRECTORS, etc.) rather than individuals. Same underlying CUSTOMER Table; tailored Projection for corporate workflows.

q: Projections operate as a…
tip: Projections act as the system's Presentation & Logic CONFIGURATION ENGINE — controlling presentation (UI layout, field properties), validation hooks (pre/post programs, hot fields), and governance (AUTH.REQUIRED, audit triggers). They translate the underlying Table definition into role-aware, workflow-aware user-facing experiences.

q: What does Server-Driven UI prevent the need for?
tip: Server-Driven UI (SDUI) eliminates the need for HARD-CODED FRONTEND FORMS or client redeployment when forms change. The server defines the form (in a Projection); the client renders whatever schema is returned. Edit the Projection; users see the new form immediately on next request.

q: What does the Resolution Waterfall in Projections guarantee?
options: The most relevant active draft is shown
tip: The Resolution Waterfall in Projections guarantees users always see the MOST RELEVANT state: NAU drafts surface first (so users continue their in-flight work), then LIV (current authorized), then HIS (history), then ARC (archive). Without this priority, users might see stale LIV when they had a pending draft.

q: True or False: Projections can override DDD field rules.
options: True — e.g. making optional fields MANDATORY in a specific projection
tip: Projections CAN override DDD field rules — that's the whole point of Field Property Overrides. The DDD might say a field is optional; the Projection can make it MANDATORY for THIS specific role/scenario. Or NOINPUT (read-only here). Or HIDDEN. The DDD is the underlying truth; Projections are the per-context restrictions.

q: Projections render forms via…
options: Dynamic JSON schemas returned by the backend
tip: Projections deliver dynamic JSON schemas describing the form — fields, labels, layout, validation rules. The frontend renders the schema. Admins editing the Projection (via config) instantly change what users see, without redeploying any client code. This is Server-Driven UI in action.

q: How are loan-origination flows organized in projections?
options: Associated Projections as Tabs (General, Interest, Collateral)
tip: A loan origination flow with 200+ fields shouldn't be one massive form. Use Associated Projections — split into Tabs like General / Interest / Repayment / Collateral / Documents. Each tab is its own Associated Projection. Users navigate intuitively; cognitive load is manageable.

q: Visual Diff between draft and baseline is used during…
tip: The Diff Engine (visual diffs between draft and baseline) is most useful during AUTH — the Authoriser sees exactly what fields the Initiator changed, accelerating review and accuracy. It's also used in audits — comparing two historical versions to understand evolution.

q: WYSIWYG drag-and-drop designer is used in…
tip: WYSIWYG drag-and-drop designer is the tool admins use to compose Projection layouts — drag fields onto the canvas, group them into Sections, arrange Tabs (Associated Projections). No coding required. The output is a Projection configuration; the runtime applies SDUI.

q: What does Defaulting from TODAY do?
options: Sets a date field to the current bank date
tip: Defaulting from TODAY in a Projection pre-fills a date field with the current Bank Date (from the DATES Table). So new transactions get today's date automatically. Other defaulting variables: USER (the logged-in user's ID), COMPANY (the current company code).

q: Hot Fields trigger logic at which moment?
options: ON.CHANGE / onBlur — when a user tabs out
tip: Hot Fields trigger on the onBlur event — the moment the user tabs/clicks out of the field. The associated plugin runs immediately, performing validation or auto-population. This is INSTANT feedback before the user submits the form.

q: Pre-hooks run…
tip: Pre-hooks fire BEFORE the operation commits (INPUT, AUTH, etc.). They can perform cross-field validations or synchronously call external APIs (like Credit Check, KYC). If a pre-hook rejects the operation, nothing is written. They're the gate keepers — synchronous, blocking, run-before-commit.

q: Post-hooks run…
