tip: Profiles can restrict visible data by COMPANY (legal entity), branch (subdivision), and unit (department/desk). A teller at the Lisbon branch of Subsidiary A would only see records belonging to that scope — not records from Subsidiary B or from a different branch. This makes the system safe for use across complex multi-entity banking groups.

q: What three operational areas does the security model expressly support segregation between?
tip: Standard banking segregation: front-office (customer-facing — tellers, RMs), back-office (operations, settlement, reconciliation), and administration (system config, user management). Different Profiles per area prevent any one person from controlling the full lifecycle of a transaction — a core principle in regulated banking.

q: If a user is configured as Initiator for FUNDS.TRANSFER, can she also AUTHORISE her own transfer?
options: No — the Profile typically prevents the same user from authorising her own input
tip: The Profile system is designed to prevent this. Even if a user has both INPUT and AUTHORISE permissions on FUNDS.TRANSFER, the system distinguishes WHOSE record is being authorised: the original Initiator (recorded in INPUTTER) cannot also be the Authoriser (recorded in AUTHORISER). This is enforced at the framework level — not a per-user toggle.

q: An ENQUIRY-only Profile means…
options: The user can read but cannot create/edit/approve
tip: An ENQUIRY-only Profile grants read access via Enquiries — the user can run reports, view lists, drill into details — but cannot INPUT (create), AUTHORISE (approve), REVERSE, or otherwise mutate the database. Useful for compliance officers, internal auditors, and read-only management dashboards.

q: Profiles often differ between which staff types?
tip: Typical Profiles by role: TELLER (INPUT on transactions, ENQUIRY on accounts, no AUTHORISE), MANAGER (everything the teller has + AUTHORISE for relevant amount thresholds), ADMIN (system configuration, USER table, override management). Each is scoped to specific Tables, operations, and data visibility.

q: What audit fields trace the Initiator and Authoriser of a record?
tip: Every Record's audit block records two arrays: INPUTTER (the user IDs and departments that created/modified each version) and AUTHORISER (the user IDs and departments that approved each version). They're multi-valued because a Record may go through many input/approve cycles over its lifetime — the audit captures the FULL chain.

q: Why are INPUTTER and AUTHORISER multi-valued?
options: To capture the full chain when multiple edits/approvals occur over the record's lifecycle
tip: A Record can be amended many times — each amendment is a new INPUT/AUTH cycle. The multi-value audit fields preserve every cycle: who edited the v1, who approved v1, who edited v2, who approved v2, and so on. This is critical for compliance investigations: 'who approved the third amendment to this loan and when?' Multi-value INPUTTER/AUTHORISER answer that.

q: A 'Profile' in CBS is most analogous to which industry concept?
tip: Profiles in CBS implement role-based access control (RBAC) — the industry-standard model where permissions are granted to roles, and users inherit them via role assignment. Where CBS goes further than basic RBAC is in the dimensional scoping: by Table, Company, branch, unit, AND operation type.

q: Per the glossary, a Product is best described as…
options: A configurable blueprint that defines structure, properties, and conditions required to create an Arrangement (Contract)
tip: A Product is a BLUEPRINT — not an actual customer contract. It defines the structure, properties, and conditions (interest formula, fee schedule, repayment terms) that a Contract must follow. One Product (e.g. 'Gold Mortgage') can spawn thousands of Contracts (one per customer who takes out that mortgage). Products can also be abstract — declaring conditions that descendant products must concretize.

q: A Contract (= Temenos ARRANGEMENT) is…
options: An individual customer contract created from a Product in Product Factory
tip: A Contract is the per-customer instance born from a Product. So if 'Gold Mortgage' is the Product, then 'John Smith's 20-year Gold Mortgage signed on 2024-03-01 at 4.5%' is a Contract. The Contract inherits everything from the Product but can override negotiated parameters (rate, margin, dates, limits) specific to that customer.

q: Which parameters can a Contract override from its parent Product?
options: Rate, margin, dates, limits — negotiation parameters
tip: The Contract can override parameters that legitimately vary per customer: interest rate, margin, start/end dates, limits, etc. These are the 'negotiation' parameters. Other Product attributes — like which Components apply, the underlying schedules' structure, accounting rules — are inherited from the Product and aren't typically overridden at the Contract level.

q: What is a COMPONENT in Product Builder?
options: A structural element of a product or arrangement (e.g. Interest, Charge, Schedule, Limit, Payment, Insurance)
tip: A Component is one piece of a Product's structure — like a building block. Each Component controls a specific aspect: Interest defines the interest calculation, Charge defines fees, Schedule defines repayment installments, Limit defines credit limits, Payment defines payment processing, Insurance defines bundled insurance. A complete Product is composed of multiple Components.

q: A complete product definition is formed by…
tip: A Product isn't a single thing — it's an assembly of Components. A Mortgage Product typically includes an Interest Component, a Charge Component (fees), a Schedule Component (monthly repayments), a Limit Component (credit limit), maybe an Insurance Component. Together, they define how the Product behaves end-to-end.

q: Which is NOT a typical Product Component?
tip: Typical Components per the glossary: Interest, Charge, Schedule, Limit, Payment, Insurance. Username is a security concept (USER Table) — it has nothing to do with product structure. Components are always business/financial building blocks of the contract behavior.

q: Where in the Temenos vocabulary does 'COMPONENT' map?
tip: CBS COMPONENT = Temenos PROPERTY (uppercase, as a Product Builder element). Beware the naming collision: Temenos uppercase 'PROPERTY' is a Component; lowercase 'Property' is a Product Attribute. CBS uses two distinct words ('Component' for the building block, 'Product Attribute' for the reusable behavior class) to avoid this confusion.

q: A Product can serve as a parent entity with…
options: Abstract conditions that must be redefined in the descendant
tip: Products can be ABSTRACT — they declare conditions but leave them to be filled in by descendant Products. For instance, a base 'Mortgage' Product might declare 'rate must be set' without specifying what; concrete descendants like 'Gold Mortgage' and 'Diamond Mortgage' override that with actual rates. This abstraction is the basis of PARENT.PRODUCT inheritance.

q: PARENT.PRODUCT inheritance allows a child to…
options: Inherit attributes/conditions and override only the delta
tip: If you set a Product's PARENT.PRODUCT to another Product, the child inherits all the parent's attributes and conditions — you only specify the DELTA (the differences). To create 'Diamond Mortgage' identical to 'Gold Mortgage' except 0.5% lower rate, define Diamond with PARENT.PRODUCT=Gold and just override the rate. Reduces drift and configuration time dramatically.

q: Why does the system distinguish Product (blueprint) from Contract (instance)?
options: Reuse + versioning: one Product spawns many Contracts; effective-dated changes to Product don't disrupt active Contracts
tip: Reuse: one Product blueprint serves thousands of customers' Contracts. Versioning: Products are versioned by PRODUCT.ID + EFFECTIVE.DATE — new versions can be scheduled to take effect at a future date without disrupting Contracts already in force under the old version. This is the same pattern as having a class and many instances in OO programming.

q: Which Contract field would NOT typically be inheritance-overridable?
tip: Negotiable per-Contract: rate, margin, start/end dates, limits. NOT negotiable per-Contract: the architectural framing — Product Class (top tier — ACCOUNT/LENDING/DEPOSIT), the set of Components used, the system-level accounting hooks. These are inherited rigidly from the Product hierarchy and shape the Contract's overall behavior.

q: A Personal Loan with a special promotional rate for a VIP customer would be modeled as…
options: A new Product for that one customer
A standard Contract from the Personal Loan Product, with rate overridden at the Contract level
tip: Standard pattern: use the existing Personal Loan Product but override the negotiated rate in that VIP's Contract. Creating a new Product per customer would explode product count, complicate maintenance, and defeat the purpose of the Product/Contract distinction. Per-customer negotiation belongs at the Contract level.

q: A new bank product launching with the same Interest behavior as an existing one benefits from…
options: Modular Building-Block Assembly — share the Interest Component, no duplication
tip: Components are reusable building blocks. The same Interest Component can be plugged into many Products. So if the bank tweaks the Interest Component (fixes a rounding bug, adds a new pattern), every Product using that Component instantly benefits — no per-Product code change needed. This is one of the Product Factory's biggest leverage points.

q: If a bank enhances the 'Charges' Component, which Products benefit?
options: Every Product using that Component instantly benefits
tip: Modular Building-Block Assembly means a Component change propagates everywhere the Component is used. Enhance Charges once — and every Product that includes Charges picks up the improvement automatically. Single change, system-wide benefit. This is the key advantage of composition over per-Product hardcoding.

q: The Contract Operation mechanism is responsible for…
options: Executing state changes and processing actions on Contracts created from Products
tip: Contract Operation = the system engine that runs defined business actions (Activities) on Contracts: applying a payment, restructuring a loan, accruing interest, etc. The key point is that Contracts can ONLY be modified via Contract Operations / Activities — not by free-form Field editing. This ensures every change is rule-checked, audited, and lifecycle-aware. Temenos calls this an 'Activity'.

q: What is Soft Accounting?
options: Flexible accounting configuration framework within Product Factory allowing financial postings to be adjusted via configuration rather than code
tip: Soft Accounting is the framework that lets banks adjust how transactions post to the General Ledger via CONFIGURATION rather than code changes. The same business event (e.g. interest accrual) can produce different GL postings in different countries, products, or regulatory regimes — all defined as config. This is critical for international banks operating under multiple accounting standards (IFRS, US GAAP, local regulators).

q: Why is Soft Accounting valuable to a bank?
options: It adapts accounting rules to products/entities/regulators without changing code
tip: Without Soft Accounting, every accounting rule change (new regulator, new product variant, new local treatment) would require a developer to modify code, test, and deploy — slow and risky. Soft Accounting reduces these to config changes that take minutes and don't touch the codebase. This is essential in a regulated industry where rules change frequently.

q: Soft Accounting lives within which architecture?
tip: Soft Accounting is part of the Product Factory — accounting behavior is configured per Product (and inheritable to Contracts) so that different Products can post differently. The Product Factory's component-based design naturally accommodates Soft Accounting as another configurable Component layer.

q: A bank operating in two regulatory environments uses Soft Accounting to…
options: Tailor financial postings per environment through configuration
tip: Concrete example: the same loan disbursement might post one set of GL entries under IFRS rules (used by the European subsidiary) and a different set under US GAAP rules (used by the American subsidiary). Soft Accounting handles this through configuration — same business event, different postings, no code change.

q: What is a Consolidation Key?
options: A configurable aggregation key used for financial consolidation and reporting
tip: A Consolidation Key is an aggregation tag attached to financial postings to group them for reporting. By configuring keys (by region, currency, product type, etc.), the bank can roll up data for consolidated financial statements (e.g. group-level P&L). The key structure is configurable so each bank can build the consolidation hierarchies relevant to their org chart and regulatory reporting needs.

q: Why are Consolidation Keys configurable?
options: So banks can define their own aggregation hierarchies for reporting
tip: Different banks consolidate differently. A retail bank might consolidate by branch and product family; a multinational might consolidate by country and currency; an investment bank might consolidate by desk and trading book. Configurable Consolidation Keys let each bank define its own dimensions without vendor changes.

q: What is an ACTIVITY in Product Factory?
options: A business action performed on a Contract within the Product Factory
tip: An Activity (= Contract Operation in CBS internals) is a defined business action you can perform on a Contract — examples: apply a repayment, accrue interest, draw down on a facility, restructure terms, close the contract. Every Contract change MUST go through an Activity; you cannot just edit a Contract's Fields directly. This enforces consistency, audit, and business-rule integrity.

q: Why can't Product Factory components be modified arbitrarily?
options: Because all changes must occur through defined Activities — preserving controlled, auditable contract evolution
tip: Free-form editing of a Contract's components would bypass business rules and break the audit trail. Activities encapsulate every legitimate change as a defined operation with rules, validations, and ledger impacts. The result: every Contract change has a known reason, was performed by a known user, and respected all applicable business rules — vital for regulated banking.

q: Compared with traditional Table editing, the Activity model is…
options: More controlled — every change is a defined, auditable action
tip: Traditional Tables let users edit fields directly through Projections (with 4-Eyes authorisation). The Product Factory Activity model goes further: even within a Contract you can't free-edit fields — you must invoke a defined Activity. This narrows the surface area dramatically and ensures every change to a Contract is a 'known move' with predictable consequences.

q: What is an OVERRIDE in CBS?
options: A structured, non-blocking validation exception generated when a soft business rule is breached
tip: An Override is a SOFT validation exception. When a rule is breached that isn't a hard stop (e.g. customer exceeded their soft limit by a small amount), the system raises an Override. The transaction is held in unauthorized status (in NAU) and can only proceed once a user with the right OVERRIDE.CLASS approves the override. Hard validation errors block entirely; Overrides allow approved exceptions.

q: Which class controls who can approve a specific Override?
options: OVERRIDE.CLASS — security classification for override approvers
tip: OVERRIDE.CLASS is a security classification on the Override itself, defining which Profiles/Roles are entitled to approve THIS kind of Override. So a low-severity Override might be approvable by a team lead (OVERRIDE.CLASS = TEAM_LEAD); a high-severity one might need a senior manager (OVERRIDE.CLASS = SENIOR_MANAGER). This gives fine-grained control over exception approval.

q: Override vs hard validation failure — what's the difference?
options: Override = soft rule, can be approved by authorised user; hard failure = blocks entirely
tip: Hard validation failure: the transaction is invalid (e.g. wrong format, missing mandatory field, illegal date) — no one can approve their way past it; the user must fix the data. Soft rule breach → Override: business judgment is needed; a properly classified user can approve the exception and let the transaction proceed. Both are recorded; Overrides additionally create an audit-trail of who approved the exception.

q: An Override-pending transaction is held in which status?
tip: The transaction sits in unauthorized status (typically in the NAU file as UNAU or INAU) until an appropriately classed user reviews and approves the Override. Only then does the normal AUTH flow continue. Until override approval, the transaction cannot affect the GL or LIV state — it's effectively quarantined.

q: Why use Overrides instead of blocking the transaction outright?
options: To allow legitimate exceptions with controlled, auditable approval — preserving business flexibility
tip: Banking has many soft rules that should usually hold but occasionally need to bend for legitimate reasons — a long-standing customer slightly exceeding their limit, an early payment that breaks a schedule rule, a manual fee waiver for goodwill. Overrides let these proceed WITH proper approval rather than blanket-blocking. Every override is logged with who approved it, when, and under which OVERRIDE.CLASS — full audit trail.

q: What is a Schema in CBS?
options: The logical structure of a Table defining the set of Fields, their types, attributes, and data-handling rules
tip: The Schema is the logical metadata model of a Table — it defines what Fields exist, what types they are (text, number, date, etc.), what attributes they have (mandatory, multi-value), and what data-handling rules apply (validation, defaulting, formatting). The Dynamic Data Dictionary (DDD) operates on the Schema; Projections reference it; the runtime uses it to validate every read and write.

q: The Schema serves as the…
tip: The Schema is the source-of-truth metadata for the Table. It says 'these are the Fields, this is their shape, these are the rules'. Everything else — UI rendering (via Projections), validation (via DDD), reporting (via Enquiries) — references the Schema. Change the Schema and all downstream consumers respect the change.

q: An OVERRIDE in CBS is best described as a…
tip: Overrides are per-transaction exceptions — they don't change the underlying rule for other transactions, they just grant approval for THIS one. The rule remains in force; the exception is logged. This is fundamentally different from changing the validation rule in DDD (which would affect all future records).

q: Hard-coded accounting vs Soft Accounting — which is easier to change for new regulations?
options: Soft Accounting — config change rather than code change
tip: Hard-coded accounting requires developers to modify code, run tests, release through change management — typically weeks. Soft Accounting reduces it to a configuration change validated by business analysts — typically hours. In a regulated industry where rules can change at a regulator's whim, this agility is enormous: lower cost, lower risk, faster compliance.

q: Why does the Product Factory enforce 'no arbitrary edits' on Components?
options: To preserve audit, business rules, and lifecycle integrity — every change is a defined Activity
tip: If users could directly edit a Contract's Interest Component or Schedule, the audit trail would be ambiguous ('who changed what why?'), business rules could be silently violated, and lifecycle invariants could break. Forcing every change through a defined Activity means every Contract change has a known cause, a recorded actor, and respects all rules. It's the heart of why Product Factory is suitable for banking.

q: An admin needs to know how a Table validates data. Where should she look?
options: The Schema (metadata model defining Fields, types, validation) — accessed via DDD
tip: The Schema in the Dynamic Data Dictionary is the source of truth for validation behavior. It tells you each Field's type, allowed values, mandatory/optional status, default values, and any attached validation routines. Because the DDD is hot-swappable, admins can also update validation rules here and they take effect immediately — no recompilation.

q: A retail bank wants the General Ledger to be assembled at runtime from transactional data rather than being a rigid pre-defined chart of accounts. This describes…
tip: The Virtual General Ledger (VGL) is a CBS concept where the GL is LOGICAL and constructed dynamically from underlying accounting entries using Consolidation Keys — instead of being a pre-defined chart of accounts. Benefits: dynamic grouping, support for multiple accounting standards (e.g. local GAAP + IFRS simultaneously), and creation of GL accounts only when needed (reducing data volume).

q: A 'Transaction-Driven Accounting Model' means…
options: Accounting entries are typed manually each night
Accounting entries are generated automatically as a direct result of business transactions
Accounting depends on hand-stamped paper slips
tip: Transaction-Driven Accounting means every business transaction (a payment, a fee, an interest accrual) AUTOMATICALLY raises the corresponding accounting entries in real time. The transaction is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH — operations and accounting cannot diverge. Compare with the legacy approach where accountants entered figures manually or batched them overnight.

q: Which Table in CBS stores entries reflecting account-level movements (Assets and Liabilities)?
tip: STMT.ENTRY (Statement Entry) stores account movements affecting Asset and Liability balances. Key fields include ACCOUNT.NO (which account moved), DEBIT.CREDIT (which side), AMOUNT.FCY/AMOUNT.LCY (foreign and local currency amounts), CURRENCY, TRANSACTION.CODE, BOOKING.DATE/VALUE.DATE/PROCESSING.DATE, ACCOUNT.OFFICER/CO.CODE, and CONSOL.KEY for consolidation.

q: Which Table holds entries that affect the bank's Profit & Loss categories?
tip: CATEGORY.ENTRY (Category Entry) stores entries that hit specific P&L categories — typically used for fees, interest income/expense, and other revenue/cost items. Key fields include CATEGORY (the specific P&L category), DEBIT.CREDIT, AMOUNT.FCY/AMOUNT.LCY, TRANSACTION.CODE, and CONSOL.KEY. Together STMT.ENTRY and CATEGORY.ENTRY cover the full double-entry posting.

q: A bank cashier processes a customer wire transfer at 14:30 today. The funds will be available on the recipient's account on the next business day. Which date attribute tracks the 'when does this affect the account balance for calculation' moment?
tip: VALUE DATE = the date from which the funds count for interest and balance calculations. It can differ from when the entry was BOOKED. BOOKING DATE = when the entry was recorded in the ledger. TRANSACTION DATE = when the customer initiated. PROCESSING DATE = when the system actually executed. For interest accrual, Value Date matters most.

q: A securities trade is executed Tuesday, settled Thursday. The bank records the trade on Tuesday for risk management but the cash moves Thursday. The date capturing the original deal moment is…
tip: TRANSACTION (TRADE) DATE captures when the deal was originally struck — the contractual moment. SETTLEMENT (often = Value Date) is when funds actually move. BOOKING DATE is when the entry was recorded in the books. Each date can be different; together they capture the full timeline for compliance, risk, and accounting purposes.

q: Which architectural property makes consolidation flexible enough to support local GAAP and IFRS simultaneously over the same transactions?
tip: CONSOLIDATION KEYS are parameterized criteria attached to each accounting entry — they can encode product, currency, sector, category, residency, balance type. Different KEY combinations roll up entries into different views: one consolidation key set rolls up by local GAAP, another by IFRS. Same underlying entries, multiple parallel reports — no parallel postings required.

q: Which is NOT one of the four primary date types tracked on STMT.ENTRY/CATEGORY.ENTRY records?
tip: The four primary date types are: BOOKING DATE (when recorded in ledger), VALUE DATE (when funds affect balance/interest), TRANSACTION/TRADE DATE (when initiated), and PROCESSING DATE (when system actually ran the operation). Tax Date is a separate concept handled by tax modules — not one of the canonical posting dates.

q: A bank operating across 6 countries needs to produce balance sheets per country, per currency, and per product line — all from the same underlying entries. Which architectural feature enables this?
tip: Consolidation Keys let one set of accounting entries be sliced multiple ways for reporting. By encoding country, currency, product line as dimensions in the key, the same transaction data rolls up differently per dimension. Six countries × multiple currencies × multiple product lines — no data duplication, just configurable key-based aggregation.

q: Comparing legacy vs modern accounting: which is characteristic of the LEGACY (Direct/Manual) approach?
options: Granular, instant visibility per line item
Summary-based, delayed visibility; high error risk; slow month-end-focused processing
tip: The Legacy/Direct/Manual approach is summary-based and delayed — accountants typed in or batched entries periodically. Errors were common (manual input), audit trails were hard to reconstruct, and visibility lagged. The modern Transaction-Driven approach reverses all these: automated, real-time, granular, low-risk, with seamless audit.

q: Soft Accounting allows…
options: Only IT to change accounting rules via code
Banks to adjust postings via CONFIGURATION rather than code changes
Customers to write their own entries
tip: Soft Accounting moves accounting decisions from CODE into CONFIGURATION. The bank defines, per product or event, what debit/credit movement, posting details, transaction codes, categories, and balance types should be raised — all in config. Hard parts (amounts, currencies, at least one essential entry reflecting the operation's nature) remain pre-set; the flexible parts are configurable.

q: An AC.EVENT is best described as…
options: A defined accounting event (like INTEREST-ACCRUAL or LOAN-DISBURSEMENT) that triggers postings
tip: AC.EVENT represents a specific accounting trigger — e.g. WITHDRAWAL-FEE, ACCRUAL-INTEREST-DAILY, DRAWDOWN-DISBURSE. Each AC.EVENT is mapped (via AC.ALLOCATION.RULE) to the specific debit/credit movements and balance impacts it generates. The chain: AC.EVENT → AC.ALLOCATION.RULE → AC.POSTING.DETAILS → Accounting entries (STMT.ENTRY / CATEGORY.ENTRY).

q: AC.ALLOCATION.RULE defines…
options: How an AC.EVENT translates into specific debit/credit movements, codes, categories, and balance types
tip: AC.ALLOCATION.RULE is the heart of Soft Accounting. For each AC.EVENT, the rule specifies the debit/credit movement, posting details (transaction code, category, balance type) and may multiply by amounts/currencies. The rule transforms a single accounting event into one or more accounting entries — completely via configuration.

q: Why does the system insist that 'at least one essential entry type reflecting the nature of the operation' must be pre-set, not soft-configurable?
options: To ensure the accounting outcome at least respects fundamental business semantics — e.g. an interest accrual MUST raise entries between Accrual A&L and P&L
tip: Soft Accounting can adjust codes, posting details, and add extra entries — but the system requires that the CORE entry that captures the economic nature of the operation be hard-coded. For instance, an INTEREST accrual must hit Accrual A&L vs P&L. Otherwise, banks could mis-configure the system into hiding losses or misrepresenting income. The constraint preserves accounting integrity.

q: An 'AL Key' (in consolidation) refers to…
options: Asset & Liability consolidation key, used to group Asset/Liability entries
tip: AL Keys are Asset & Liability consolidation keys — they group STMT.ENTRY records that affect Asset/Liability balances. The complementary PL keys group CATEGORY.ENTRY records hitting P&L categories. Consolidation distinguishes contingent (off-balance-sheet) from non-contingent items, supporting both balance sheets and P&L reports from the same underlying entries.

q: Real-time posting (vs batch posting) in modern CBS means…
options: Accounting entries are created INSTANTLY during transaction execution, not at end-of-day
Postings require manual approval per entry
tip: In a real-time posting engine, the moment a business transaction (e.g. a $1,250 wire transfer for a Berlin SME client) is authorized, the accounting entries are raised immediately. No batch wait. Financial position is instantly accurate. The Legacy batch model would create the entries overnight — delaying visibility and audit.

q: An entry in CATEGORY.ENTRY for 'Interest Income on Personal Loans' would primarily be classified as…
options: A P&L (Profit & Loss) category entry — Income side
tip: CATEGORY.ENTRY holds P&L entries. 'Interest Income on Personal Loans' is income earned by the bank — it credits a P&L income category. The matching Asset-side debit (interest receivable or cash) is in STMT.ENTRY. Together they form the double-entry posting; the consolidation engine assembles them into balance sheet and P&L reports.

q: In a contemporary CBS approach, when a bank teller authorizes a customer's $850 cash deposit, what happens to the GL?
options: The transaction immediately raises STMT.ENTRY/CATEGORY.ENTRY records and the virtual GL is updated in real time
An accountant types it in tomorrow
An email is sent for manual posting
tip: Transaction-Driven Accounting: the moment the deposit is authorized, the posting engine raises STMT.ENTRY records (debit Cash, credit the customer's account liability) and the virtual GL is automatically rolled up via Consolidation Keys. No manual entry, no waiting, no batch. The transaction is its own accounting event.

q: Why do modern systems decouple accounting from business processes?
options: So contract logic (e.g. loan lifecycle) doesn't need to know GL specifics — both can evolve independently
tip: Tight coupling means every loan-product change risks breaking accounting, and every accounting change risks breaking products. Decoupling lets contracts emit AC.EVENTs; the Soft Accounting layer maps these to postings independently. Accounting rules can be tuned for new regulators without touching loan code; loan products can evolve without rewriting GL rules.

q: A 'Reporting (GL)' layer sits at the TOP of the accounting pyramid; what sits at the BOTTOM?
tip: The pyramid is: BOTTOM = Raised entries (atomic STMT.ENTRY / CATEGORY.ENTRY records, one per business event) → MIDDLE = Consolidation by Keys (aggregation via Consolidation Keys) → TOP = Reporting (GL) (the consolidated views: balance sheet, P&L, regulatory reports). Operations can be Manual (UI) or Automatic (API).

q: In Trade Dating vs Value Dating accounting, what's the practical difference?
options: Trade dating records by when the deal was struck; Value dating records by when funds count for balance/interest
Trade dating is monthly; Value dating is yearly
tip: Trade dating accounting recognizes the transaction on the deal date (when it was contractually agreed). Value dating accounting recognizes on the value date (when funds actually count for interest/balance). For securities, Trade and Value are usually different (T+2 settlement). For deposits/loans, value date often matters more. Choice depends on regulatory framework and product type.

q: STMT.ENTRY and CATEGORY.ENTRY are similar — both hold accounting entries. Their KEY difference is…
options: STMT.ENTRY is for online; CATEGORY.ENTRY is for offline
STMT.ENTRY tracks Asset/Liability movements at the ACCOUNT level; CATEGORY.ENTRY tracks P&L category movements
STMT.ENTRY is for staff; CATEGORY.ENTRY is for customers
tip: STMT.ENTRY = Statement Entry, tied to a specific ACCOUNT (its balance moves). It contains ACCOUNT.NO and tracks Asset/Liability impacts. CATEGORY.ENTRY = tied to a P&L CATEGORY (Globus P&L categories 1-19999). It tracks revenue/expense impacts. Both have CONSOL.KEY for aggregation, but they sit on different sides of the bank's books.

q: Consolidation Keys can be either…
options: Pre-defined (fixed) OR customized (banks define their own at initial setup)
tip: Consolidation Keys come in two flavors: PRE-DEFINED (vendor-provided standard keys for common dimensions like product, currency, sector) and CUSTOMIZED (banks define additional dimensions during setup — e.g. internal cost-center hierarchies, custom risk pools). Together they let each bank produce reports tailored to its org chart, regulators, and analytics needs.

q: A 'TRANSACTION.CODE' on STMT.ENTRY identifies…
options: What KIND of accounting transaction this entry represents (e.g. cash deposit, fee accrual, interest payment)
tip: TRANSACTION.CODE is a classifier identifying the type of accounting movement — e.g. 'CASH-DEP' for cash deposits, 'CHK-WD' for cheque withdrawals, 'INT-ACR' for interest accruals. Reporting uses TRANSACTION.CODE to filter and group entries — e.g. 'show me all fee accruals in May 2026 across all branches'.

q: What does the AMOUNT.FCY field on STMT.ENTRY store, compared with AMOUNT.LCY?
options: AMOUNT.FCY = the amount in the Foreign (transaction) Currency; AMOUNT.LCY = the equivalent in the Local (company) Currency
tip: Multi-currency banking requires dual-currency tracking. AMOUNT.FCY is the original transaction amount in its native currency (e.g. €4,200 for a Madrid transaction). AMOUNT.LCY is the equivalent in the bank's local currency (e.g. £3,750 if the bank's local books are in GBP). The CURRENCY field tells you what FCY is denominated in.

q: 'Synthetic and analytical accounting' (as STMT.ENTRY supports) refers to…
options: Two views of the same data: synthetic (aggregated by category) and analytical (granular by account/contract)
tip: Synthetic accounting = summarized/aggregated view (totals per category). Analytical accounting = detailed view (per account, per contract). STMT.ENTRY supports BOTH because it captures both account-level details AND category-level groupings. CATEGORY.ENTRY supports synthetic accounting only (no account-level granularity).

q: Why does a bank prefer GL accounts to be CREATED only when required (rather than all pre-existing)?
options: Reduces unnecessary data volume — keeps the chart of accounts lean and dynamic
tip: Pre-creating thousands of empty GL accounts wastes storage and clutters reports. By creating accounts only when actually needed (driven by Consolidation Key combinations encountered in real entries), the system stays lean. Reports show only meaningful accounts; admins manage less; queries run faster. This is a core advantage of the Virtual GL approach over rigid chart-of-accounts.

q: If a Swiss subsidiary reports under local GAAP while the Group reports under IFRS, a Virtual GL handles this by…
options: Maintaining two parallel sets of postings
One set of underlying entries, multiple consolidation key configurations rolling up differently per standard
tip: The Virtual GL's killer feature: ONE source of accounting entries, MANY parallel views via different consolidation key configurations. Local GAAP rolls up by one set of keys; IFRS rolls up by another. Both views are always in sync because they reference the same underlying entries. No parallel postings, no reconciliation, no risk of divergence.

q: Soft Accounting allows the bank to ADJUST which of the following per AC.EVENT?
options: Transaction codes, posting details, added extra entries — flexibility to fine-tune accounting outcomes
tip: Soft Accounting's adjustability covers: TRANSACTION CODES (refine the classification), POSTING DETAILS (which sub-accounts/categories), and ADDED EXTRA ENTRIES (add supplementary postings for specific scenarios). Amounts and currencies are NOT adjustable in Soft Accounting — they're driven by the underlying business transaction; you can't make an event post different amounts than it economically represents.

q: A bank's 'CONSOL.KEY' field on STMT.ENTRY for a fixed-rate mortgage interest accrual on a USD denominated loan in the Sydney branch might look like…
options: A compound string encoding dimensions like product code, currency, sector, category, country, balance type
tip: CONSOL.KEY is a composite string encoding multiple dimensions: product code, currency, sector, category range, country/residency, balance type — separated by delimiters. The reporting layer groups entries sharing key prefixes for any required slice (e.g. 'all USD mortgage entries in Australia', 'all fee-revenue entries across Europe').

q: The proposed CBS target architecture is described as 'metadata-driven'. What does this mean in practice?
options: Behavior — fields, validations, workflows, UI — is configured via metadata records rather than hardcoded in code
Metadata is the only thing stored
tip: A metadata-driven architecture stores the rules of behavior in configurable metadata records (TABLE.DEF for schema, FIELD.DEF for field rules, PROJECTION.DEF for UI, VALIDATION.PRGM for rules). Code becomes a generic engine that READS metadata and acts on it. Result: changing behavior = editing metadata = no code change, no recompile, no redeploy.

q: What is the role of an 'Activity Orchestrator' in the target architecture?
options: Centrally manages the entire business transaction (locking accounts, validating limits, posting accounting) in a deterministic atomic sequence
tip: The Activity Orchestrator replaces distributed 'choreography' (events bouncing across microservices via message bus) with central 'orchestration' — one engine drives the full transaction (lock, validate, post, calculate) atomically. Eliminates race conditions, simplifies debugging, and prevents the half-committed state problem of saga-style designs.

q: Why is atomicity (all-or-nothing) of a complex banking transaction (e.g. a loan restructure) critical?
options: To prevent partial failures where balances update but schedules don't — which would corrupt data
tip: A loan restructure typically updates many things: principal, schedule, interest rate, accounting entries. If only some succeed and others fail, the loan is in a corrupt state. Atomicity (via the Activity Orchestrator) ensures the entire operation either fully completes or fully rolls back — leaving no half-updated mess that requires manual reconciliation.

q: In the legacy distributed-sagas architecture, what frameworks are typically used to pass events between microservices?
tip: The legacy approach uses Axon Framework (which dictates a specific CQRS/Event Sourcing model) and RabbitMQ for messaging. The downside: distributed transaction failures, complex Saga management, and difficult rollback when events fail mid-flight. The target architecture replaces this with Apache Kafka for unified event streaming plus the Activity Orchestrator for transactional coordination.

q: Why is Apache Kafka preferred in the target architecture for asynchronous messaging?
options: Persistent + replayable + high-throughput event log — single source of truth for async data flows
tip: Kafka offers: PERSISTENT (events are stored, not just delivered), REPLAYABLE (consumers can re-read history), and HIGH-THROUGHPUT (scales to millions of events/sec). For a bank with webhooks to external partners and many internal subscribers, this is critical. Compared with Axon's restrictive programming model + RabbitMQ's transient messages, Kafka simplifies the architecture significantly.

q: The Application (Table) Registry as a kernel-level component acts as…
options: A single source of truth for all executable programs and data tables — enforcing 'no program runs and no data is accessed without registration'
tip: The Application Registry is the 'constitution' of the system — every program is registered (PROGRAM Registry) and every Table is registered (FILE Registry). The kernel refuses to execute unregistered code or access unregistered Tables. This central control eliminates uncontrolled data creation, enforces classification (INT/FIN/CUS), and supports the 4-Eyes Principle at the platform level.

q: The native $HIS and $NAU file architecture provides which key benefit for auditing?
options: Audit metadata (INPUTTER, AUTHORISER, timestamps) is STRUCTURALLY MANDATED inside every record at the storage level — no risk of message-bus failures losing logs
tip: In the legacy approach, audit logs were sent over a message bus to a separate Audit service. If the bus dropped a message, the audit trail had a gap. Native $HIS (History) and $NAU (Not Authorized) files structurally embed audit data INSIDE every record — INPUTTER, AUTHORISER, timestamps are committed atomically with the data itself. No async log loss possible.

q: Schema-less Extension (LOCAL.REF) lets developers and admins do what?
options: Inject new fields DYNAMICALLY at runtime without recompiling microservices or altering DB schemas
tip: LOCAL.REF is the kernel mechanism for extending records with bank-specific fields. New fields are declared in metadata (LOCAL.REF.TABLE) and immediately appear in the record without database DDL or code recompile. Critical for regulatory adaptability — when a new compliance field is mandated, the bank adds it via config the same week, not via a 6-month project.

q: How does the target architecture handle the bank's day-end batch processing?
options: Generic 'automated processes' that compete with online traffic
A dedicated, multi-threaded 5-Phase COB engine tailored for massive financial datasets
tip: The target introduces a purpose-built 5-Phase Close of Business (COB) engine — multi-threaded, dedicated to batch processing of interest accruals, statements, reports, etc. It DECOUPLES the system date from the business date — so batch processing doesn't degrade online API performance. Compare with legacy 'automated processes' that competed for the same DB resources as user transactions.

q: Why is the legacy approach of GraphQL BFF (Backend-For-Frontend) doing data-stitching across PostgreSQL and MongoDB a problem?
options: Heavy joins across two DB engines make the BFF a performance bottleneck
tip: When the BFF has to query PostgreSQL for Party data, then MongoDB for Accounts, then merge them in memory, it becomes the slowest link in every request. The target moves data-stitching back into the core database engine (where joins are optimized) and uses Next.js BFF purely for frontend view optimization — drastically reducing API latency.

q: What is the benefit of using Istio Service Mesh in the target Kubernetes stack?
options: Out-of-the-box mTLS (zero-trust security between pods) plus intelligent traffic routing
tip: Istio provides automatic mutual TLS (mTLS) between all pods — meaning all internal microservice traffic is encrypted by default with zero application-level configuration. It also handles intelligent routing (canary deploys, A/B tests, retries). Combined with the observability stack (Jaeger for tracing, Mimir for metrics), it gives operators full visibility into a distributed banking platform.

q: HashiCorp Vault is introduced in the target architecture for…
options: Dynamic secrets management and certificate rotation
tip: HashiCorp Vault manages secrets dynamically — database credentials, API keys, TLS certificates — generating short-lived credentials and rotating them automatically. This replaces the legacy 'config-level secret management' (static secrets in config files, easy to leak). For a regulated bank, dynamic secrets dramatically reduce attack surface.

q: Ceph is introduced in the target architecture for…
options: High-performance distributed block and S3 object storage — esp. for Document Management (binary blobs)
tip: Ceph handles large binary blobs (scanned KYC documents, statements, contracts) on a distributed storage cluster — offloading them from the core transactional databases. This preserves IOPS (disk operations/sec) for high-speed transactional workloads. Legacy systems often store blobs inside the DB itself, dragging down core banking performance.

q: Which observability stack does the target architecture use?
tip: The target observability stack: Prometheus (metrics scraping), Mimir (long-term metrics storage), Alloy (telemetry collection), Jaeger (distributed tracing), Logstash (log aggregation). Together they give developers and operators deep insight into the running system — for instance, Jaeger can pinpoint exactly which microservice and DB query is causing a latency spike.

q: Why does the target architecture introduce a SINGLE definition framework for products (vs services-per-channel)?
options: Ensures consistent product logic and behavior across ALL channels (mobile, web, branch, API) without duplication
tip: Legacy systems often have product logic duplicated in different services per channel — leading to drift (mobile loan calculator gives different result than branch one). A single product definition framework (the Product Factory) ensures one source of truth — every channel computes the same interest, same fees, same schedules. No drift, no reconciliation.

q: Activity History (in the target Product Factory) stores…
options: Detailed records of exactly what changed (e.g. 'Interest Rate changed from 5% to 6%'), who did it, and when
tip: Activity History captures every change to an Arrangement as a structured event: which Activity ran, who triggered it, what fields changed, before and after values. This is far richer than a generic 'audit log' — it's specifically tied to the business semantics of the product. Regulators love this because it answers 'who reduced the rate?' or 'who waived the fee?' immediately.

q: Why store business rules ON the Product definition rather than in UI or COB code?
options: Centralization reduces 'spaghetti code' and makes the system easier to upgrade and maintain
tip: If a rule like 'charge a fee if balance < $500' lives in UI code AND in COB code, you have to update both whenever the threshold changes — and risk drift. Storing the rule ON the Product centralizes it: one place to update, every channel respects it. The target architecture explicitly puts rules in Product definitions, not scattered across UIs or batch jobs.

q: Multi-company data segregation in the target architecture is enforced…
options: Built-in separation of data across INT, FIN, and CUS classifications at the kernel level
tip: Legacy approach often used HTTP headers or loose conventions to segregate company data — error-prone and bypassable. The target architecture bakes segregation INTO the kernel via classifications: INT (Installation, globally shared), FIN (Financial, strictly per-company), CUS (Customer, configurable). The kernel REFUSES to mix data across boundaries — making leakage architecturally impossible.

q: Edit Locking in the target architecture is described as a 'single active record per entity with collision detection and controlled lock handling'. Which file enables this?
tip: $NAU is the singleton file holding in-flight changes (one record per entity ID). When user A's draft is in $NAU for record X, user B trying to edit X gets a controlled 'Record Locked' error. This native edit-locking is enforced at the file level — no distributed lock manager required, no race conditions possible.

q: Why does the target architecture place 'Centralized Governance' in the system kernel rather than in applications?
options: Reduces complexity and prevents 'spaghetti logic' across many apps each enforcing their own version of rules
tip: Kernel-level governance = ONE place implements segregation, 4-Eyes, edit locking, audit. Applications just use these primitives. Scattered governance (different rules in different apps) causes drift, gaps, and 'whose-version-is-right?' chaos. Centralization simplifies upgrades, audits, and security reviews — the kernel is the trusted layer.

q: Compare legacy 'fragmented brokers' (Axon + RabbitMQ) vs target unified event streaming. What's eliminated?
options: The steep learning curve of Axon's CQRS/Event-Sourcing programming model + the dual-broker complexity
tip: Axon imposes a rigid CQRS/Event Sourcing programming style — developers must learn it before contributing. Plus RabbitMQ handled standard messaging. Two brokers = two operational stacks = two failure modes. Kafka alone handles everything (events, webhooks, async messaging) with a simpler model — fewer moving parts, lower cognitive load.

q: An 'External Event Publisher' (webhooks) in the target architecture is built on top of which broker?
tip: Kafka's persistence and replayability make it ideal for webhooks: even if an external partner's endpoint is down, events sit in Kafka and are delivered when they recover. Replay enables re-sending if a partner needs to recover from their outage. High throughput handles thousands of partners. Compare with RabbitMQ's transient delivery (lost if not consumed).

q: Configurable validation framework (target arch) means validations are…
options: Defined and executed dynamically based on metadata — admins can change rules without code changes
tip: Instead of compiling validation logic into code, the framework reads validation rules from metadata (e.g. 'this field must be a positive amount in [USD, EUR, GBP]'). Admins update rules; the framework picks them up at runtime. Release cycles shrink from months to minutes for rule changes — critical for regulatory compliance.

q: Virtual Tables (in the target DDD) reduce the need for…
options: Physical Tables holding small static dictionaries (Risk Levels, Statuses, Country Codes for one form)
tip: Without virtual tables, every small reference list ('Risk Level: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW') becomes its own physical Table — clutter, schema bloat, migration overhead. Virtual Tables centralize all such lists in ONE physical table (EB.LOOKUP), distinguished by compound keys. The DDD reports 90% reduction in physical schema for simple reference data.

q: Per the target architecture, what's the benefit of 'Effective Dating & Versioning' on products?
options: Each product record is effective-dated — supports historical tracking AND scheduling future product launches/condition updates
tip: Effective dating means each product version has start/end dates. The bank can schedule a fee structure change to take effect on a future date (e.g. 2027-01-01) — when that date arrives, the system automatically switches versions. Historical reports always use the version that was effective at the report date. No weekend deployments to flip versions.

q: The 5 primary triggers for Activities (in target architecture) include…
options: User (manual), Transaction (external apps), Scheduled (COB), OFS (external systems), Secondary (cascade from primary)
tip: Activities can originate from: USER (a manager manually edits), TRANSACTION (an external app submits a payment that drives an activity), SCHEDULED (the COB engine triggers daily accruals), OFS (Open Financial Service — external system integrations), or SECONDARY (one Activity automatically chains to another). All five paths converge through the same Activity Orchestrator.

q: Why is 'separating system date from business date' important for COB?
options: Lets the COB engine process the day's batch without users seeing the date change until business reopens
tip: System date = the OS clock; business date = the 'banking day' the system considers current. If COB takes 4 hours overnight, you don't want users seeing the business date 'flicker' mid-batch. Decoupling means the business date advances only when COB completes successfully. Plus 24/7 operation: batches can run while branches in other time zones operate.

q: Determinism (in 'centralized transaction orchestration') means…
options: Same inputs always produce the same outputs in the same order — making behavior predictable and debuggable
tip: Deterministic orchestration: given the same starting state and the same operation, the result is identical every time, in a predictable sequence. Compare distributed sagas: events bouncing across microservices may arrive in different orders, causing race conditions and non-deterministic outcomes. Determinism makes debugging tractable — you can reproduce any production issue locally.

q: Per the target architecture, the LOCAL.REF mechanism (Schema-less Extension) primarily serves which need?
options: Adding bank-specific or region-specific fields (e.g. a unique national tax ID format) without changing core code
tip: Different countries have different fields: a German bank may need a unique tax identifier (Steueridentifikationsnummer); a Singapore bank needs an FIN ID; a Japanese bank needs a different ID format. LOCAL.REF lets the local team add these fields via metadata without modifying the vendor's core code — preserving the upgrade path.

q: Compared to a generic distributed event-driven approach, what kind of debugging advantage does the centralized Activity Orchestrator provide?
options: Simpler debugging — the full transaction lives in one place; no need to trace events across multiple services and brokers
tip: In a saga-based system, debugging a failed transaction means tracing the event chain across services, brokers, and timing windows — often impossible to reproduce. A centralized Orchestrator runs the whole transaction in one process with a clear sequence. Failures have a single stack trace, a single timeline, and are 100% reproducible. Operations team's life is dramatically easier.

q: Which Table holds system-wide immutable rules like floating-point precision, cache settings, and password requirements?
tip: SPF = System Parameter File. It holds GLOBAL system parameters that govern installation-wide behavior — precision rules, cache settings, password complexity requirements, default session timeouts. Editing SPF changes the bank's 'operational constitution'. The COMPANY Table holds per-company config; SPF is for installation-wide.

q: A bank running multiple Companies in one installation typically has…
options: One Lead Company and many Branch companies
tip: Standard topology: ONE Lead Company per installation + many Branch companies underneath. The Lead Company is the primary autonomous banking entity; Branches share its tables and operate centrally. The COMPANY Table holds this topology. Initial deployments often start with just the Lead Company and add Branches as the bank scales.

q: The PGM.FILE (Program File) and FILE.CONTROL Tables together implement…
options: The Application (Table) Registry — central code governance and data table classification
tip: PGM.FILE = Program Registry (registers all executable programs and enforces execution types). FILE.CONTROL = File Registry (registers logical data tables with their classifications: FIN, INT, CUS, plus links to physical storage including $HIS and $NAU files). Together they form the kernel-level Application Registry — the system's 'service directory'.

q: What does the EB.LOOKUP Table provide that complements STANDARD.SELECTION?
options: Virtual table support — central storage of dynamic lookup lists (dropdowns) defined via metadata
tip: STANDARD.SELECTION holds the DDD field-level metadata (types, validation routines). EB.LOOKUP holds VIRTUAL TABLES — the dropdown values, reference lists, and dynamic dictionaries. Together they let admins define both 'what kind of field this is' (STANDARD.SELECTION) and 'what values it can take' (EB.LOOKUP) entirely via metadata.

q: What does the CURR.NO (Current Number) audit Field record?
options: The current sequential version number of the record (e.g. 1 = original, 2 = first amendment, etc.)
tip: CURR.NO is the version counter for a record. CURR.NO=1 means it's the original input; CURR.NO=3 means there have been 2 amendments. Combined with INPUTTER, AUTHORISER, DATE.TIME, the audit block provides the complete forensic timeline of every action on a record from creation onward — meeting SOX and Basel requirements without custom code.

q: A 'LOCAL.REF.TABLE' record extends a core application (e.g. CUSTOMER) by…
options: Injecting new bank-specific fields into the LOCAL.REF segment of the application; STANDARD.SELECTION automatically rebuilds to make them queryable
tip: LOCAL.REF.TABLE is the mechanism to add custom fields without modifying the core. Define the new fields (e.g. PREFERRED.LANGUAGE, REGIONAL.TAX.ID) in LOCAL.REF.TABLE; the system injects them into LOCAL.REF on CUSTOMER records; STANDARD.SELECTION auto-rebuilds so the fields are immediately queryable via enquiries. Upgrade-safe — vendor upgrades preserve custom fields.

q: The SUB.ASSOC.CODE attribute is used to…
options: Group custom fields into associated multi-value sets (e.g. building a custom 'Address Block' with several related fields)
tip: Custom multi-value blocks (e.g. multiple addresses each with street, city, postal code) need their member fields kept index-aligned. SUB.ASSOC.CODE designates fields as members of the same association — so adding a new index shifts all members consistently. Same principle as core associated multi-value fields, but for bank-defined extensions.

q: Why does the system DECOUPLE the business date from the server clock?
options: Ensures financial integrity regardless of the physical clock — the COB batch is the only entity that advances the business date
tip: If the server clock advances, the business date shouldn't automatically — interest, schedules, and reports all key off the business date. The DATES Table (storing TODAY, LAST.WORKING.DAY) is updated ONLY by Close of Business (COB). This means batch processing controls when 'a new day starts' in banking terms. Branches in different time zones see the same business date globally.

q: The system supports a 3-tier holiday calendar structure. Which is NOT one of the three tiers?
tip: The 3 tiers are: OFFICIAL.HOLIDAY (country legal holidays — for interest calc rules), BRANCH.HOLIDAY (when the physical branch is open — can differ from official, e.g. a branch open Saturday morning), and BATCH.HOLIDAY (when COB runs — usually 365/year). 'Customer Holiday' isn't a thing — customers don't dictate the bank's calendar.

q: Branch opening on a Saturday WITHOUT advancing the financial business date is supported via…
options: The hierarchical calendar — BRANCH.HOLIDAY differs from OFFICIAL.HOLIDAY/BATCH.HOLIDAY
tip: If your branch in São Paulo wants to open Saturday morning to serve customers, you set BRANCH.HOLIDAY to NOT mark Saturday as a holiday — but OFFICIAL.HOLIDAY still marks it as non-business. So customers can transact, but interest accrual, value dating, and ledger close treat it as a non-business day. Best of both worlds.

q: CURRENT.DAY status can be NORMAL, RESTRICTED, or CLOSED. What does RESTRICTED indicate?
options: The branch is open even though it's officially a holiday or weekend — accept transactions but with adjusted financial treatment
tip: NORMAL = regular business day. CLOSED = no operations at all. RESTRICTED = an unusual day where the branch operates but the official calendar marks it as non-business. Useful for special events, regulatory exceptional days, or branch-specific Saturday openings. The status influences how transactions value-date themselves.

q: EB.TIME.ZONES handles…
options: Multi-region processing — adjusting audit timestamps based on each Company's location relative to the server
tip: A bank with branches in Singapore, London, and New York needs audit timestamps that make sense to each local team. EB.TIME.ZONES lets the system normalize timestamps based on each Company's location. The Sydney branch sees their actions in Sydney time; HQ sees consolidated logs in HQ time. Same underlying data, locale-aware presentation.

q: Date Conventions for handling scheduled dates that fall on holidays include Forward, Backward, and Modified Following. What does 'Modified Following' mean?
options: Move to the next business day, but if that crosses a month-end, move BACKWARD to the previous business day instead
tip: Modified Following is a common banking convention: if a scheduled date (e.g. month-end interest accrual) lands on a holiday, MOVE FORWARD to the next business day — UNLESS that moves into the next month, in which case move BACKWARD. Ensures payments stay in the original month. Used widely in FX, derivatives, and structured products.

q: In the LANGUAGE Table, Language 1 is reserved for…
tip: LANGUAGE 1 = English (by convention, system-wide reserved). Languages 2, 3, ... are bank-defined per region (French, Mandarin, Spanish, Arabic, etc.). The reservation means all base error messages, dictionary terms, and system documentation have a defined Language 1 fallback. Bank-specific languages extend the base.

q: DYNAMIC.TEXT handles application messages containing variable data (like 'Account & has insufficient funds'). What's the role of the '&' placeholder?
options: Replaced by actual values (e.g. the specific Account ID) at runtime when the message is translated and displayed
tip: DYNAMIC.TEXT messages have placeholders (often '&') that the system substitutes at runtime — so the user sees 'Account 12345 has insufficient funds' (with the actual account ID). The placeholder mechanism works across all configured languages: the English template 'Account & has insufficient funds' has counterparts in French, German, etc., each with the placeholder. The runtime substitutes the actual value.

q: If a Japanese branch needs Japanese characters validated correctly, which mechanism enables this?
options: ASCII.VAL.TABLE / ASCII.VALUES — define valid characters per language, supporting multi-byte characters like Japanese, Chinese
tip: ASCII.VAL.TABLE (despite the name) supports multi-byte and Unicode characters per language. Japanese (with hiragana, katakana, kanji), Chinese, Arabic, and Hebrew all need different valid-character sets. The validation engine reads ASCII.VAL.TABLE per LANGUAGE and rejects characters outside the allowed set — preventing data corruption or display issues.

q: The CURRENCY framework supports up to 99 distinct currency MARKETS. Why?
options: Different exchange rates per channel — e.g. Cash/Notes (market 10) vs Interbank (market 1) for the same currency pair
tip: Same currency, different rates depending on context: an Interbank market (market 1) has tight spreads for institutional deals; Cash/Notes (market 10) has wider spreads to cover handling costs; Wire transfer market might be different again. 99 markets gives enough headroom for any bank's product/channel mix. Driven by CURRENCY.MARKET configuration.

q: CURRENCY.PARAM enforces…
options: IMMUTABLE structural standards (Numeric Code, Decimals) across a multi-company environment — same code, same decimals, every Company
tip: Even in a multi-company installation where different Companies might use different exchange rates (CURRENCY.MARKET), the STRUCTURE of a currency must be uniform — Numeric Code (ISO 4217), decimal places. CURRENCY.PARAM enforces this immutably so reconciliation across Companies is reliable. JPY always has 0 decimals everywhere; USD always has 2.

q: QUOTATION.CODE is used for low-value currencies. Example use case?
options: Quoting rates per 100 or 1000 units (e.g. for JPY/KRW), making the rate display human-readable
tip: Some currencies have very low per-unit value (Japanese Yen, Korean Won, Vietnamese Dong). Quoting 'USD/JPY = 0.0067' is awkward; 'USD/100 JPY = 0.67' or 'USD per 1000 KRW = 0.85' is easier to read. QUOTATION.CODE tells the system to quote in units of 100 or 1000 for these currencies. Calculations always use the underlying rate; display uses the friendly quotation.

q: USER.SMS.GROUP enables…
options: Role-based provisioning — define 'Job Types' (Teller, Manager, etc.) with bundled permissions; assign users to groups instead of editing each user
tip: USER.SMS.GROUP (SMS = Security Management System, not text messages) is the role-based permissions engine. Define a TELLER group with INPUT rights on certain Tables; users in that group inherit the rights. Updating the group updates all members. Bulk permission management — critical at scale. Users link to groups via '@GROUPNAME' in their profile.

q: The 4-Level RBAC matrix dimensions are…
tip: 4-Level RBAC controls access along four axes: (1) Company — which branches/legal entities; (2) Application — which Tables/Versions; (3) Function — what verbs (INPUT, AUTHORISE, DELETE, REVERSE, SEE); (4) Field — data-level masking (e.g. hide salary from regular tellers). All four together = 'zero trust' permission model.

q: Temporal access logic in the USER Table allows the bank to enforce…
options: Permitted system usage by time of day (START.TIME, END.TIME) and day of week (ALLOWED.DAYS), including holiday-specific rules
tip: Temporal restrictions: a teller might be allowed access only Mon-Fri 8am-6pm; an external auditor only on specific days. START.TIME / END.TIME / ALLOWED.DAYS enforce this — login outside the window is rejected. Holiday-specific extensions allow exceptions (e.g. operations team can log in on a holiday for emergency support). Reduces attack surface during off-hours.

q: Data Access Control (DATA.ACCESS) is used to…
options: Restrict a user's ability to view/process specific records based on field-level values (e.g. preventing standard staff from seeing 'Staff Accounts')
tip: DATA.ACCESS is row-level + field-level security. Example: 'Staff Accounts' (the personal accounts of bank employees) are identified by a specific category range. Standard tellers should NOT see them (privacy + integrity). DATA.ACCESS lets you define this filter so the standard teller's enquiries never return Staff Accounts — even though the same Table holds them.

q: EB.ROLE manages…
options: Specific roles or legal capacities (Signatory, Proxy, Guarantor) for staff and external parties involved in customer relationships
tip: EB.ROLE captures legal/business capacities a party can have in a relationship: SIGNATORY (can sign on behalf of), PROXY (legally represents), GUARANTOR (backs a credit). These roles are critical for compliance, contract enforceability, and AML. They sit alongside the standard CUSTOMER profile, attached via the RELATION table.

q: Password complexity (Minimum length, required uppercase/lowercase/numeric/special character counts) is defined where?
options: Centrally in the SPF — system-wide rules everyone must respect
tip: SPF (System Parameter File) holds the password syntax rules — minimum 12 characters, at least 1 upper, 1 lower, 2 numeric, 1 special. The system enforces these centrally; no app can override. Changing the SPF rule applies to next password change for every user. Critical for meeting regulatory standards (e.g. NIST guidelines) consistently.

q: AUTOLOG.TIME.MINS enforces…
options: Auto-logout for users holding RECORD LOCKS — ensuring locks don't persist forever if a user walks away
tip: If a user holds a $NAU draft (record lock) and walks away, the lock would block other users indefinitely. AUTOLOG.TIME.MINS automatically logs out users who've been inactive WHILE holding locks — releasing the locks for others. Distinct from TIME.OUT.MINUTES which is general inactivity logout. Prevents the 'forgotten draft' from blocking team productivity.

q: The PROTOCOL file captures…
options: Security violations and unauthorized access attempts — even if individual user logging is off, violations are ALWAYS recorded here
tip: PROTOCOL is the security audit file — failed login attempts, unauthorized access tries, password resets, security setting changes. Critically, it's MANDATORY: even if individual user logging is disabled, security violations still land in PROTOCOL. The EOD report generator produces a regular PROTOCOL report for compliance and forensic readiness.

q: OVERRIDE.CLASS.DETAILS allows the bank to set…
options: Specific value thresholds per override (e.g. amounts > 10,000 USD require manager-class approval; below that, supervisor-class is enough)
tip: Different overrides require different authority levels. OVERRIDE.CLASS.DETAILS encodes thresholds: 'unauthorised overdraft up to $5,000 = supervisor; up to $50,000 = branch manager; above $50,000 = regional director'. The override flow automatically routes to the right tier based on the amount — no human triage needed. Smarter exception handling.

q: DISPO.OFFICER is the…
options: Designated officer to whom an override of a specific class/type is routed for review/approval
tip: When an override is raised, it doesn't just go to 'somebody' — it goes to the DISPO.OFFICER assigned to that override class/type. Each officer has a defined competence area and hierarchy level. Combined with DISPO.ITEM (the override record itself), the system manages an exception WORKFLOW with defined responsibility — no overrides get lost in inboxes.

q: DISPO.ITEM tracks…
options: Individual override records — preventing final authorization of the master transaction until ALL associated DISPO.ITEMs are marked 'APPROVED'
tip: A transaction might breach multiple soft rules and raise multiple overrides. Each override is a DISPO.ITEM in the queue. The master transaction CANNOT be authorized until every DISPO.ITEM is marked APPROVED by an authorized officer. This ensures exceptions aren't accidentally skipped — every override is explicitly handled.

q: DEPT.ACCT.OFFICER stores…
options: The bank's organizational hierarchy — divisions, departments, account officers/relationship managers — linked to users and to accounting entries
tip: DEPT.ACCT.OFFICER is the org-chart Table. It defines business units, divisions, departments, and individual Account Officers/Relationship Managers, with reporting lines to parents. Every accounting entry can carry an ACCOUNT.OFFICER code, letting the bank consolidate financial activity by department for P&L attribution and accountability.

q: Why does the system maintain a MNEMONIC.CUSTOMER mapping?
options: Provides a human-readable alternate key for CUSTOMER records — users can search by mnemonic instead of long customer numbers
tip: Customer numbers are usually long, opaque sequences (e.g. CUSTOMER 178293461). Tellers don't memorize those. MNEMONIC.CUSTOMER gives each customer a short, memorable alternate key — like 'SMITH-J-LON-1' for John Smith in London. Search by mnemonic; the system resolves to the underlying customer number. Quality-of-life feature for high-volume teller work.

q: The RELATION Table manages…
options: Complex party relationships (Corporate Groups, Parent/Child, Signatory, Proxy, Guarantor) — automatically generating reverse-relationship records
tip: Banking parties have many possible relationships: corporate parent/subsidiary, beneficial ownership, guarantor, signatory, joint account holder, power-of-attorney. RELATION captures these as bidirectional records — defining one direction automatically creates the reverse (parent → child also creates child → parent). Critical for 360-degree risk views and KYC.

q: The AML.CHECK field captures…
options: Persistent state of whether AML screening has been sent, accepted, or rejected for this customer
tip: AML.CHECK has values like SENT, YES (passed), NO (failed/blocked). It's the customer-level state of AML screening — alongside AML.RESULT (awaited/positive/negative). These persistent fields drive workflow: positive results may block account opening; awaited results trigger KYC re-checks. Combined with AUTO.NEXT.REVIEW.DATE, they automate KYC lifecycle management.

q: FATCA and MiFID are…
options: Regional regulatory compliance frameworks (FATCA = US tax; MiFID = EU investor protection); the system has fields for each
tip: FATCA = Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (US law requiring foreign banks to report US persons' accounts to the IRS). MiFID = Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (EU regulation governing investment-services suitability). The system has dedicated fields (US TIN for FATCA, investor risk profile for MiFID) — config rather than custom development per regulation.

q: The AA.PRODUCT.MANAGER service enforces…
options: The Product Factory's 3-stage lifecycle (Design → Proof → Publish) — including the Proof quality gate that blocks misconfigured products
tip: AA.PRODUCT.MANAGER is the orchestrator of the product lifecycle. Design = sandbox configuration. Proof = compile-and-validate; checks inherited conditions resolve, mandatory attributes exist, conditions don't conflict. Publish = commits to PRODUCT.CATALOG for live origination. Failed Proof blocks publication — protecting customers from misconfigured products that could mis-price loans or skip required accounting.

q: ARR.LINK options include TRACKING, NON.TRACKING, and CUSTOMER.TRACKING. What does CUSTOMER.TRACKING enable?
options: Allows negotiation on certain attributes (per-customer rate, fee waiver) while the OTHER attributes still track product changes
tip: ARR.LINK governs how Arrangements (customer contracts) follow Product updates. TRACKING = inherits all product changes (mass updates apply). NON.TRACKING = fixed at creation, never changes. CUSTOMER.TRACKING = hybrid — negotiated attributes (e.g. a special VIP rate) stay fixed, but other attributes still follow product updates. Best of both worlds for negotiated customers.

q: The AA.ARR.PRODUCT.TRACKER service is responsible for…
options: AUTOMATICALLY updating Arrangement condition records when a Product is re-published — propagating changes to thousands of live contracts in one click
tip: Re-publishing a Product (e.g. changing a fee) should propagate to all live customer contracts using that Product. AA.ARR.PRODUCT.TRACKER scans Arrangements, identifies those linked TRACKING (or partially TRACKING) to the updated Product, and applies the changes. One product update = mass contract maintenance, no batch script writing needed.

q: EB.DUPLICATE.TYPE supports duplicate prevention by…
options: Letting the bank define field combinations (e.g. Debit Account + Amount + Value Date) to form a composite key — duplicates detected against this key
tip: Duplicate prevention is critical — accidentally processing a $250,000 wire twice is catastrophic. EB.DUPLICATE.TYPE lets you define what counts as a duplicate per transaction type: a payment might dup-check on (Debit Acct + Amount + Value Date); a loan disbursement on (Customer + Amount + Date). Composite keys catch both technical (same message ID) and functional (same business intent) duplicates.

q: ARCHIVE / ARC.GENERIC handle…
options: Migrating high-volume historical records (old STMT.ENTRY, closed transactions) from live files to read-only archive ($ARC suffixed tables) based on PURGE.DATE or RETENTION.PERIOD
tip: Over time, live tables accumulate millions of records and slow down. The ARCHIVE engine, driven by ARC.GENERIC.REQUEST, migrates old data to $ARC suffixed tables (e.g. STMT.ENTRY$ARC). The migration uses PURGE.DATE (absolute) or RETENTION.PERIOD (rolling window) rules. Archive Tables are read-only and live on cheaper storage — preserving compliance access while keeping live tables fast.
